title: Phase 1: Types, Schema, and Licensing Lock
description: Foundation phase: establish all shared types, finalize formula definitions, resolve word-list licensing, and lock configuration decisions before any implementation begins.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: PHASE OVERVIEW: Foundation establishes the contracts and decisions that all subsequent phases depend on. Nothing in Phases 2-5 can begin until the types are defined, formulas are specified, word-list licensing is resolved, and Cloudflare constraints are documented. This phase produces zero runnable code but makes all future code deterministic -- implementers should be able to work from Phase 1 outputs alone without ambiguity. KEY OUTPUTS: (1) readability-types crate with all shared structs/enums (AnalysisResult, Score, FormulaError, ConsensusGrade, etc.), (2) canonical formula definitions with exact coefficients pinned, (3) word-list licensing clearance (Dale-Chall 3000 and Spache 1000 are distributable), (4) fixture corpus plan (30+ texts with genre/difficulty diversity), (5) Cloudflare Worker limits locked (128KB max body, 30ms CPU budget, 10 batch max), (6) Cargo workspace structure. DEPENDENCY GRAPH: All Phase 1 beads can run in parallel except .8 (workspace scaffold) which depends on .1 (types crate) since it creates the workspace that types lives in. Phase 2 cannot start until .1 (types), .2 (formula defs), .5 (licensing), and .8 (workspace) are complete.

title: Create readability-types crate (types + warning codes + error codes)
description: Shared request/response/options/error types with serde derives. Includes: AnalyzeRequest, AnalyzeOptions, AnalysisResult, BatchRequest, BatchResponse, Score, TextStats (serialization view), FormulaError enum, WarningCode enum (EMPTY_TEXT, SHORT_TEXT, FEW_SENTENCES, NO_SENTENCE_TERMINATOR, SMOG_SHORT_SAMPLE, SPACHE_SCOPE, NON_ENGLISH_UNSUPPORTED, HTML_MALFORMED, MARKDOWN_CODE_IGNORED, TOKENIZER_AMBIGUITY, SYLLABLE_ESTIMATE, NORMALIZED_SCORE, DEBUG_OUTPUT_CAPPED, TEXT_HASH_DISABLED, PARTIAL_RESULT, NO_VALID_METRICS, FORMULA_ERROR) with severity levels and Display impl. No deps on other workspace crates. Warning codes are stable API contracts — once shipped, they do not change.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: This crate defines ALL request/response/options/error types as plain Rust structs with serde derives. It has zero deps on other workspace crates — it's the leaf of the dependency graph that everything else imports. REASONING: Separating types enables (1) future TypeScript SDK generation via ts-rs or specta without pulling in the full engine, (2) OpenAPI spec generation from the same source of truth, (3) clean compile-time boundaries so worker doesn't transitively depend on phf/word-list data. KEY TYPES: AnalyzeRequest, AnalyzeOptions, AnalysisResult, BatchRequest, BatchResponse, Score, Warning, TextStats (serialization view — not the internal computation struct). CONSIDERATION: The internal TextStats struct in readability-core uses Option<usize> for lazy tiers, but the serialized response type uses concrete values (computed or omitted). Don't conflate the two.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .4 (warning-code registry). Warning codes are Rust enum types with serde derives — they live IN the types crate as WarningCode alongside FormulaError, Score, and AnalysisResult. Not a separate deliverable.

title: Finalize canonical formula definitions
description: Lock the exact formula math for all 8 metrics from academic sources. Document coefficient values, variable definitions, and edge-case behavior (division by zero, negative results).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: All 8 formulas must be defined from their canonical academic sources — NOT from any specific library implementation. Sources: Flesch (1948), Kincaid et al. (1975), Gunning (1952), Coleman & Liau (1975), McLaughlin SMOG (1969), Smith & Senter ARI (1967), Dale & Chall (1948, revised 1995), Spache (1953, revised). REASONING: We removed DaveChild compatibility mode entirely. There is ONE correct formula for each metric. This task locks the exact coefficients, variable definitions, and edge-case behavior (what happens at 0 sentences? 0 words?). DELIVERABLE: A table in a markdown file or code comments listing each formula with its exact equation, source citation, and decision on edge cases (e.g., SMOG returns FormulaError::DivisionByZero when sentences=0). CRITICAL: The SMOG formula has a common implementation error where 3.1291 is placed inside the multiplication by 1.043. The canonical form is: 1.043 * sqrt(polysyllables * 30/sentences) + 3.1291. The addend is OUTSIDE the multiplication.

title: Generate OpenAPI spec from types
description: Set up OpenAPI spec generation from readability-types structs. Ships with v1 — not deferred. Enables auto-generated client SDKs and API documentation tooling.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: OpenAPI spec generated from readability-types structs. Ships with v1.0 — this was explicitly moved from v1.2 in the PRD revision because any API product without a spec is incomplete on day one. REASONING: OpenAPI enables auto-generated client SDKs (every language), API docs tooling (Scalar, Redocly), contract testing, and partner integrations — all at zero ongoing maintenance cost since the spec is derived from the same Rust types. APPROACH: Use utoipa or a similar derive-based approach on the types crate, or generate from serde metadata. The spec should be served at /v1/openapi.json or generated at build time and bundled. CONSIDERATION: The spec must accurately represent all 3 response formats (full/compact/scores_only). oneOf discriminated unions may be needed.

title: Define warning-code registry
description: Enumerate all warning codes, their trigger conditions, severity levels, and human-readable messages. These are stable API contracts — changing them later is a breaking change.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Warning codes are stable API contracts — once shipped, changing a code string or its trigger condition is a breaking change for clients that parse warnings programmatically. REASONING: Defining these upfront (before implementation) forces us to think carefully about what conditions are worth surfacing and at what severity level. FULL LIST (from PRD §15): EMPTY_TEXT (error), SHORT_TEXT (info, <100 words), FEW_SENTENCES (warning, <3), NO_SENTENCE_TERMINATOR (info), SMOG_SHORT_SAMPLE (warning, <30 sentences), SPACHE_SCOPE (warning), NON_ENGLISH_UNSUPPORTED (warning/error), HTML_MALFORMED (info), MARKDOWN_CODE_IGNORED (info), TOKENIZER_AMBIGUITY (info), SYLLABLE_ESTIMATE (info, >80% rule-based), NORMALIZED_SCORE (info), DEBUG_OUTPUT_CAPPED (info), TEXT_HASH_DISABLED (info), PARTIAL_RESULT (warning, CPU budget), NO_VALID_METRICS (error), FORMULA_ERROR (warning). DELIVERABLE: A Rust enum with Display impl, serde, and associated severity + default message text.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .1 (readability-types crate). Warning codes ARE types — they are a Rust enum with serde derives living in the types crate. There is no separate deliverable; the WarningCode enum is defined alongside AnalysisResult, Score, and FormulaError in the same crate. The full list of codes is documented in the PRD section 15 and in the merged bead's description.

title: Resolve word-list licensing
description: Lock source and license strategy for Dale-Chall 3000-word list and Spache revised list. Must not introduce GPL contamination. Store provenance metadata.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Dale-Chall uses a ~3000-word 'easy word' list (words familiar to 80% of 4th graders). Spache uses a revised list of words familiar to early-grade readers. Both lists have varying availability and licensing. REASONING: GPL-contamination risk is real — some published versions of these lists are embedded in GPL-licensed projects. We need a clean-room source or a permissively licensed compilation. APPROACH: (1) Check if the original Dale-Chall 1995 list is in public domain or available under permissive terms. (2) Check academic publications that reproduce the lists. (3) If unclear, compile from multiple public-domain sources and document provenance. DELIVERABLE: data/dale_chall_easy_words.txt, data/spache_easy_words.txt, data/licenses/dale_chall.LICENSE, data/licenses/spache.LICENSE with clear provenance chain. BLOCKER: No implementation work on Dale-Chall or Spache formulas can begin until this is resolved.

title: Plan fixture corpus (30+ texts)
description: Select and source 30+ representative fixture texts spanning children's, academic, legal, health, marketing, HTML, Markdown, code-heavy, etc. Store under fixtures/texts/.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The golden corpus is 30+ texts spanning diverse genres, lengths, and input formats. Each fixture gets a stored expected-output JSON with exact counts and scores. REASONING: These fixtures serve triple duty: (1) golden regression tests, (2) insta snapshot source, (3) cross-library comparison baseline. Genre diversity matters because readability formulas behave very differently on legal text vs children's books vs code docs. REQUIRED GENRES: children's (low grade), academic (high grade), legal (long sentences), health communication (plain language standard), marketing copy, support article, press release, recipe (list-heavy), poem (short lines), HTML fragment, Markdown doc, code-heavy doc (test code-block stripping), abbreviation-heavy (tokenizer stress test), short UI copy (<30 words, triggers SHORT_TEXT), long article (>5000 words, performance boundary). CONSIDERATION: Fixture texts must not be copyrighted. Use public domain works, CC-licensed content, or write original fixtures. Store under fixtures/texts/ with corresponding expected/ outputs.

title: Lock Cloudflare config and limits
description: Finalize: 128KB single max, 1MB batch max, 10 texts/batch, 30ms CPU budget, wrangler.toml vars. These are product decisions that affect the Worker crate API surface.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: These are product decisions that constrain the Worker implementation. Once deployed, changing limits is a compatibility issue for existing clients. DECISIONS TO LOCK: (1) Max single request: 128 KB (product choice, not platform limit — keeps CPU predictable on free tier). (2) Max batch body: 1 MB. (3) Max texts per batch: 10. (4) CPU budget: 30ms (return partial on exceed). (5) Wrangler vars: READABILITY_VERSION, READABILITY_MAX_BYTES=131072, READABILITY_BATCH_MAX_BYTES=1048576, READABILITY_BATCH_MAX_TEXTS=10, READABILITY_LOG_LEVEL=info. (6) Secret: CSVKEY. (7) Compatibility date: 2026-05-25. (8) Worker name: rusty-readability. (9) Build command mirrors rusty-gateway (curl rustup + wasm32 target + worker-build --release). REASONING: Size limits are enforced BEFORE body parsing (check Content-Length first) to prevent CPU waste on oversized payloads. The 30ms CPU budget prevents pathological inputs from exhausting Worker quotas.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into Phase 3 .12 (wrangler config). The 'decide CF limits' and 'write wrangler.toml' tasks are inseparable — you write the file when you make the decisions. The decisions are documented in the merged bead's description and the PRD (sections 14 and 13.3). No reason to have a separate 'think about it' bead that produces no artifact.

title: Set up Cargo workspace and crate scaffolds
description: Create virtual workspace Cargo.toml with 4 crates: readability-types, readability-core, readability-worker, readability-cli. Set up build profile, workspace-level deps.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The workspace is a Cargo virtual workspace at the repo root with 4 member crates: readability-types, readability-core, readability-worker, readability-cli. The workspace Cargo.toml defines shared deps (serde, serde_json, thiserror) and the release profile (opt-level=z, lto=true, codegen-units=1, panic=abort, strip=debuginfo). This mirrors the rusty-gateway project structure. REASONING: Virtual workspace keeps each crate independently compilable. Shared release profile ensures consistent WASM optimization. The 4-crate split follows the dependency graph: types (leaf) → core → worker/cli (leaves).

title: Phase 2: Core Rust Engine
description: Implement the readability-core crate: preprocessing, tokenization, syllable counting, word lists, all 8 formula functions, consensus algorithm, paragraph breakdown, and hardest-sentences extraction.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: PHASE OVERVIEW: The Core Engine is the heart of the project -- a pure Rust library (readability-core) with ZERO platform dependencies that implements all readability computation. It is deliberately isolated from Cloudflare Worker concerns so it can be tested natively (fast cargo test), used in CLI tools, and potentially deployed to other platforms. This phase contains the most beads (26) because it encompasses: text preprocessing (format detection, HTML/Markdown stripping, normalization), tokenization (sentence segmentation, word splitting), syllable estimation (rules + exceptions + caching), all 8 formula implementations, word-list lookups (Dale-Chall, Spache), tiered stats computation, consensus algorithm, paragraph breakdown, and the top-level analyze API. ARCHITECTURE: Inputs flow through a pipeline: raw text -> format detection -> preprocessor -> sentence segmentation -> word tokenization -> tiered stats (Tier 0: counts, Tier 1: syllables, Tier 2: word lists) -> formulas -> consensus -> response assembly. Each stage is a separate module with clear input/output types defined in readability-types. CRITICAL PATH: Preprocessing -> tokenization -> Tier 0 stats -> formulas that need only Tier 0 (Coleman-Liau, ARI) -> Tier 1 stats -> formulas needing syllables (Flesch, FK, Fog, SMOG) -> Tier 2 stats -> formulas needing word lists (Dale-Chall, Spache) -> consensus -> paragraph breakdown. CPU budget can interrupt this pipeline and return partial results.

title: Scaffold readability-core crate
description: Create crate with module structure: preprocess/, tokenize/, syllables/, formulas/, wordlists/. Depend on readability-types. Set up lib.rs with public analyze() and analyze_batch() signatures.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: readability-core is the pure Rust engine with ZERO Cloudflare dependencies. It depends only on readability-types + utility crates (phf, bumpalo, lru, sha2, regex-lite, thiserror). REASONING: Platform independence enables (1) fast native cargo test without WASM, (2) CLI usage, (3) golden corpus scripts in native speed, (4) future deployment to non-Cloudflare targets. MODULE STRUCTURE: lib.rs exports analyze() and analyze_batch(). Internal modules: preprocess/ (detect, plain, html, markdown_lite), tokenize/ (sentence, word, abbreviations), syllables/ (rules, exceptions, cache), formulas/ (8 files), wordlists/ (dale_chall, spache), consensus.rs, interpretation.rs, warnings.rs, paragraphs.rs, stats.rs, options.rs, analyze.rs. PUBLIC API: pub fn analyze(input: &str, options: &AnalyzeOptions) -> Result<AnalysisResult, AnalyzeError>; pub fn analyze_batch(inputs: &[(&str, &str)], options: &AnalyzeOptions) -> Vec<Result<AnalysisResult, AnalyzeError>>; Each formula internally returns Result<Score, FormulaError> for per-formula error isolation.

title: Implement cross-request syllable LRU cache
description: Static RefCell<LruCache<String,u8>> with 2048 entries. Persists across requests in same Worker isolate. Zipf distribution means top-2000 words cover ~80% of text. Benchmark to ensure lookup < recomputation cost.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The cross-request LRU cache exploits the fact that Cloudflare Worker isolates are reused across multiple requests. A bounded cache persists syllable counts between requests, amortizing the most expensive per-word operation. DESIGN: static RefCell<LruCache<String, u8>> with 2048 entries. Single-threaded WASM means no Mutex needed. Stores lowercase normalized word → syllable count (u8 is enough — no English word exceeds 255 syllables). WHY 2048: English follows Zipf's law — the top 2000 most frequent words account for ~80% of running text. After a few requests, most word lookups will be cache hits. WHY LRU (not HashMap): Bounded memory. Without eviction, pathological inputs (random strings, URLs) would grow the cache unboundedly across the isolate's lifetime. BENCHMARK GATE: If cache lookup overhead exceeds recomputation for 1-2 syllable words (the majority), the cache hurts. Measure on real workloads. May need to only cache words ≥5 chars.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The syllable LRU cache persists across requests within a Worker isolate to amortize the cost of syllable estimation. English text reuses vocabulary heavily (Zipf's law: the top 100 words account for ~50% of all word occurrences). Once we compute syllables for 'the', 'and', 'information', etc., we should never recompute them in the same isolate lifetime. GOAL: Implement a thread-safe LRU cache mapping word -> syllable_count that persists across requests within the same Worker isolate. Capacity: 2048 entries (covers typical vocabulary of a heavy-use session). IMPLEMENTATION: Use the lru crate with a global static behind once_cell::sync::Lazy or std::sync::OnceLock. Thread safety via Mutex (CF Workers are single-threaded per isolate, so contention is zero -- the Mutex is purely for Rust's safety requirements). Cache key: lowercase word (String). Cache value: u8 (syllable count, max 255 -- no English word exceeds this). LOOKUP ORDER: cache.get(word) -> if Some, return cached count. If None, check exception dictionary (phf), then run rule-based estimator, then insert result into cache before returning. EVICTION: LRU eviction when at capacity. The 2048 cap ensures bounded memory (~50KB worst case: 2048 * 25 bytes avg word). PERFORMANCE: Expected hit rate after warm-up: 70-90% for typical English prose (measured in Phase 5 cache-tuning bead). Each cache hit saves ~500ns of syllable rule processing. ISOLATION: Each Worker isolate gets its own cache instance. No cross-isolate sharing (that would require Durable Objects, which is out of scope).

title: Implement word-list codegen via build.rs (Dale-Chall + Spache)
description: build.rs reads data/dale_chall_easy_words.txt (~3000 words) and data/spache_easy_words.txt (~1000 words). For each: generate common inflections (s, es, ed, ing, ly), emit phf::Set via phf_codegen. Public API: is_dale_chall_familiar(word) and is_spache_familiar(word). Both O(1) lookup, zero runtime allocation. Dale-Chall counts repeated unfamiliar words; Spache counts unique unfamiliar words (deduplicated per text). Same build.rs pattern, same inflection logic, same phf tooling — implemented together as one coherent word-list codegen module.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Dale-Chall uses a ~3000-word 'easy word' list. A word NOT on the list counts as 'difficult'. The percentage of difficult words drives the formula. IMPLEMENTATION: build.rs reads data/dale_chall_easy_words.txt (one word per line, lowercase). Generates common inflections: +s, +es, +ed, +ing, +ly, plus safe irregular forms. Emits either a phf::Set (compile-time perfect hash — O(1) lookup, but adds to bundle size) or a sorted &[&str] with binary search (O(log n), smaller bundle). BENCHMARK: Compare phf vs binary search on 3000-word set. phf is faster but adds ~30KB to WASM binary. For 3000 words, binary search on sorted array is likely fast enough (<1μs per lookup) and smaller. INFLECTION SAFETY: Only generate inflections that don't create false positives. 'live' + 'd' = 'lived' (correct). But 'lie' + 'd' = 'lied' (might not be in base list). Be conservative — only generate inflections for words where the inflected form is unambiguously derived.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The Dale-Chall formula requires checking each word against a list of ~3000 words familiar to 80% of 4th-grade students. This list must be compiled into the binary for zero-cost lookups at runtime. The original 1948 list was revised in 1995 by Chall and Dale. We use the 1995 revised list as our canonical source. GOAL: Compile the Dale-Chall 3000-word familiar list into a static perfect hash set (phf::Set) generated at build time. Zero runtime allocation, zero initialization cost, O(1) lookups. IMPLEMENTATION: build.rs reads wordlists/dale_chall_3000.txt (one word per line, lowercased, sorted alphabetically for maintainability). Generates Rust code via phf_codegen that creates a static phf::Set. Output file: dale_chall_set.rs in OUT_DIR, included via include! macro. Public API: pub fn is_dale_chall_familiar(word: &str) -> bool. WORD LIST SOURCE: The 3000-word list is widely published in educational research literature and considered public domain for computational use. Multiple open-source readability libraries ship it. We verify our list against at least 2 independent sources for accuracy. MATCHING RULES: (1) All lookups are lowercase. (2) Hyphenated words: check each component separately (both must be familiar for the compound to count as familiar). (3) Numbers are always familiar. (4) Proper nouns (detected via capitalization) are always familiar. TESTING: Spot-check 50 words known to be on/off the list. Verify set size matches expected ~3000.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .12 (Spache word list). Both Dale-Chall and Spache use identical build.rs + phf::Set pattern. Implementing them as one coherent word-list codegen module eliminates redundant setup and ensures consistent inflection logic across both lists.

title: Implement Spache word list via build.rs
description: Same approach as Dale-Chall. build.rs reads data/spache_easy_words.txt, generates inflections, emits static lookup. Spache uses unique unfamiliar words (deduplicated per text), not raw count.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The Spache Readability Formula requires a list of ~1000 words considered 'easy' for children in grades 1-3. Words NOT on this list are counted as 'unfamiliar' and increase the difficulty score. The original 1953 list was revised in 1974. We use the 1974 revised list as canonical. GOAL: Compile the Spache word list into a zero-allocation static lookup structure available at compile time. Lookup must be O(1) and the structure must not require runtime initialization. IMPLEMENTATION: build.rs reads wordlists/spache_1974.txt (one word per line, lowercased) and generates a phf::Set<&'static str>. The generated code is included via include!(concat!(env!(OUT_DIR), '/spache_set.rs')). Public API: pub fn is_spache_familiar(word: &str) -> bool. DESIGN DECISIONS: (1) phf::Set over sorted array + binary_search because phf is truly O(1) and the set is small enough that the perfect hash table fits comfortably in WASM memory. (2) All lookups are case-insensitive (caller lowercases before lookup). (3) The list includes base forms only -- we do NOT stem (matching original formula spec: 'runs' is familiar if 'run' is on the list, but we check the exact word). Actually, Spache counts derived forms as familiar, so we pre-expand common inflections in the source list. SOURCE: Public domain educational resource, no licensing concerns.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .11 (word-list codegen). Both Dale-Chall and Spache word lists use identical build.rs + phf pattern. Single bead covers both.

title: Implement Flesch formulas: Reading Ease + Kincaid Grade Level
description: Two mathematically paired formulas using identical inputs (ASL + ASW). Flesch Reading Ease: 206.835 - 1.015*ASL - 84.6*ASW (score 0-100, higher=easier). Flesch-Kincaid Grade: 0.39*ASL + 11.8*ASW - 15.59 (US grade level, higher=harder). Both need Tier 0 + Tier 1 (syllable count). Neither is clamped. If one succeeds, the other must also (same division-by-zero conditions). Implemented together because they share inputs, produce inversely correlated outputs, and are always computed from the same TieredStats snapshot.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Flesch Reading Ease (1948) is the most widely recognized readability formula. It produces a score from 0-100 where higher = easier to read. Used by US government, journalism style guides, and as a baseline comparison for all other metrics. FORMULA: 206.835 - 1.015 * (total_words / total_sentences) - 84.6 * (total_syllables / total_words). INPUTS NEEDED: Tier 0 (word count, sentence count) + Tier 1 (syllable count). No word-list lookups required. IMPLEMENTATION: fn flesch_reading_ease(stats: &TieredStats) -> Result<Score, FormulaError>. Returns FormulaError::InsufficientText if sentences < 1 or words < 1 (division by zero guard). Score is NOT clamped -- values outside 0-100 are mathematically possible for extreme texts (very simple children's text can exceed 100; dense legal text can go negative). We report the raw value and let interpretation mapping handle the label. INTERPRETATION: 90-100 (5th grade), 80-89 (6th grade), 70-79 (7th grade), 60-69 (8th-9th grade), 50-59 (10th-12th), 30-49 (college), 0-29 (college graduate). GRADE LEVEL CONVERSION: Flesch Reading Ease maps to approximate grade via: grade = -0.1 * score + 16 (linear approximation used in consensus). TESTING: Golden test with manually counted inputs to verify exact floating point output matches formula.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .14 (FK Grade). Flesch RE and FK use identical inputs, differ only in coefficients, and are always implemented together in formulas/flesch.rs.

title: Implement Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level formula
description: 0.39*ASL + 11.8*ASW - 15.59. Returns US grade level. No clamping by default. Needs Tier 1 stats.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level (1975) was developed for the US Navy to assess training manual readability. Unlike Flesch Reading Ease (higher = easier), FK outputs a US school grade level directly (e.g., 8.2 = 8th grade, 2nd month). This makes it the most intuitive formula for grade-level consensus. FORMULA: 0.39 * (total_words / total_sentences) + 11.8 * (total_syllables / total_words) - 15.59. INPUTS NEEDED: Tier 0 (word count, sentence count) + Tier 1 (syllable count). Same stats as Flesch RE but different coefficients and output scale. IMPLEMENTATION: fn flesch_kincaid_grade(stats: &TieredStats) -> Result<Score, FormulaError>. Returns FormulaError::InsufficientText if sentences < 1 or words < 1. Score is NOT clamped -- negative values possible for very simple text (e.g., 'The cat sat.' might yield grade -1.5). We report raw value; interpretation clamps display to 0-18 range. RELATIONSHIP TO FLESCH RE: These two formulas use identical inputs but produce inversely correlated outputs. We compute both from the same TieredStats snapshot, guaranteeing consistency. If one succeeds, the other must also succeed (same division-by-zero conditions). CONSENSUS ROLE: FK Grade Level is one of the primary grade-level contributors to the consensus algorithm. Its output is in the same units (US grade) as Gunning Fog, ARI, Coleman-Liau, SMOG, Dale-Chall, and Spache.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .13 (Flesch formulas). Flesch Reading Ease and Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level use identical inputs (ASL + ASW), are mathematically paired (inversely correlated), and would never be implemented separately. Single bead, single file (formulas/flesch.rs), two functions.

title: Implement Tier 1 polysyllable formulas: Gunning Fog + SMOG Index
description: Two formulas that both count polysyllabic words (3+ syllables). Gunning Fog: 0.4 * (ASL + PCW) where PCW = 100 * complex_words/total_words. Complex word exclusions: proper nouns, compounds, common suffixes (-es/-ed/-ing). SMOG: 1.043 * sqrt(polysyllables * 30/sentences) + 3.1291 (3.1291 added OUTSIDE multiplication). SMOG warns below 30 sentences (SMOG_SHORT_SAMPLE). Both share the polysyllable/complex_word_count computed during Tier 1 stats — implemented together because they consume the same per-word syllable data and are natural companions in formulas/polysyllable.rs.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Gunning Fog has a unique 'complex word' concept that requires special handling. A complex word is one with 3+ syllables EXCLUDING: (1) proper nouns (capitalized), (2) combinations of easy words (e.g., 'understand' = under+stand), (3) words made 3 syllables by common suffixes (-es, -ed, -ing). This is a HEURISTIC — there's no universal agreement on the exact exclusion rules. IMPLEMENTATION: complex_word_policy_v1: count 3+ syllable words, exclude capitalized-at-start-of-non-sentence words (proper noun heuristic), exclude words where removing -es/-ed/-ing drops below 3 syllables. The policy version is returned in the score metadata so changes are trackable. PCW = 100 * complex_words / total_words. FORMULA: 0.4 * (ASL + PCW). Note: PCW is already a percentage, and the formula uses it directly (not divided by 100 again).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Gunning Fog Index (1952) was designed by Robert Gunning for newspaper editors. It estimates the years of formal education needed to understand text on first reading. Unlike syllable-counting formulas, Fog uses 'complex words' (3+ syllables) as its difficulty proxy, which correlates well with vocabulary sophistication. FORMULA: 0.4 * ((total_words / total_sentences) + 100.0 * (complex_words / total_words)). Where complex_words = words with 3+ syllables, EXCLUDING: (1) proper nouns (capitalized mid-sentence), (2) compound words where components are simple (e.g., 'however'), (3) common suffixes -es, -ed, -ing that push a 2-syllable base to 3. INPUTS NEEDED: Tier 0 (word count, sentence count) + Tier 1 (syllable counts per word, plus complex word identification). IMPLEMENTATION: fn gunning_fog(stats: &TieredStats) -> Result<Score, FormulaError>. The complex_words count requires iterating per-word syllable data, not just totals. This means the TieredStats must expose per-word syllable counts or a pre-computed complex_word_count. DESIGN CHOICE: We pre-compute complex_word_count during Tier 1 stats collection (single pass over words, counting syllables and flagging 3+ simultaneously) rather than re-iterating. EXCLUSION RULES: Proper noun detection uses position-in-sentence heuristic (not first word + capitalized = proper noun). The suffix exclusions follow the original Gunning specification. OUTPUT: US grade level (typically 6-20+). Values above 17 indicate post-graduate difficulty.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .17 (SMOG Index). Both Fog and SMOG use polysyllabic word counts (3+ syllables) as their difficulty indicator. They share the same Tier 1 input data and are natural companions in formulas/polysyllable.rs.

title: Implement Tier 0 formulas: Coleman-Liau Index + ARI
description: The two character-based formulas that need only Tier 0 stats (no syllables, no word lists). Coleman-Liau: 0.0588*L100 - 0.296*S100 - 15.8 (letters per 100 words, sentences per 100 words). ARI: 4.71*(chars/words) + 0.5*(words/sentences) - 21.43 (alphanumeric chars including digits). Both are computationally cheapest, always completable within CPU budget, and serve as the reliable fallback in partial-results scenarios. Key difference: Coleman-Liau counts only letters (a-z); ARI counts letters + digits. Each returns Result<Score, FormulaError> with InsufficientText on zero words/sentences.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Coleman-Liau is uniquely valuable because it uses CHARACTERS, not syllables. This means it's the cheapest formula to compute (Tier 0 only) and the most robust against syllable-counting errors. It's also the best formula for demonstrating tiered computation savings. FORMULA: 0.0588 * L100 - 0.296 * S100 - 15.8, where L100 = letters per 100 words, S100 = sentences per 100 words. IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: L100 and S100 are already in the Tier 0 stats (derived from letters, words, sentences). No additional computation needed beyond basic tokenization. The formula returns a US grade level. WHY IT MATTERS FOR ARCHITECTURE: A request for metrics:[coleman_liau] should compute Tier 0 only — no syllables, no word lists. This is the key benchmark for validating tiered computation (≥30% faster than metrics:[all]).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Coleman-Liau Index (1975) is unique among readability formulas because it uses character counts instead of syllable counts. This makes it computationally cheaper (Tier 0 only -- no syllable estimation needed) and arguably more objective since character counting is deterministic while syllable counting is heuristic. FORMULA: 0.0588 * L - 0.296 * S - 15.8. Where L = average number of letters per 100 words, S = average number of sentences per 100 words. Equivalently: L = (total_letters / total_words) * 100, S = (total_sentences / total_words) * 100. INPUTS NEEDED: Tier 0 only (letters, words, sentences). This is the cheapest formula after ARI. IMPLEMENTATION: fn coleman_liau(stats: &TieredStats) -> Result<Score, FormulaError>. 'Letters' means alphabetic characters only (a-z, A-Z). Digits, punctuation, spaces are excluded from the letter count. FormulaError::InsufficientText if words < 1. DESIGN ADVANTAGE: Because Coleman-Liau needs only character-level stats, it can always be computed even if the CPU budget is exhausted before syllable counting (Tier 1). This makes it a reliable fallback in partial-results scenarios. OUTPUT: US grade level (typically 1-16). Negative values are mathematically possible for extremely simple text. CONSENSUS ROLE: Provides diversity in the consensus algorithm since it measures a fundamentally different text property (word length in characters vs. word complexity via syllables).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .18 (ARI). Both Coleman-Liau and ARI are character-based formulas needing only Tier 0 stats. They are computationally cheapest and serve as reliable fallbacks in partial-results scenarios.

title: Implement SMOG Index formula
description: 1.043 * sqrt(POLY * 30 / S) + 3.1291. Critical: 3.1291 is ADDED, not multiplied by 1.043. Warn when <30 sentences (SMOG_SHORT_SAMPLE). Mark valid=false in that case. Needs Tier 1 stats.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: SMOG (Simple Measure of Gobbledygook) has the most common implementation error of any readability formula. The canonical form is: 1.043 * sqrt(polysyllables * 30 / sentences) + 3.1291. The 3.1291 is ADDED to the product, not placed inside the multiplication. Many implementations (including DaveChild) compute 1.043 * (sqrt(...) + 3.1291), which gives a materially different result. EXAMPLE: For 30 sentences and 100 polysyllables: Correct = 1.043 * 10.0 + 3.1291 = 13.5591. Incorrect = 1.043 * (10.0 + 3.1291) = 13.6917. Difference of ~0.13 grade levels — significant for a readability score. VALIDATION: Our golden test MUST include a case that distinguishes these two forms. The test in PRD §19.1 does exactly this: assert_approx_eq!(score, 1.043 * 100_f64.sqrt() + 3.1291, 1e-9). SMOG VALIDITY: Mark valid=false and emit SMOG_SHORT_SAMPLE when sentences < 30. SMOG was calibrated on 30-sentence passages; shorter samples are unreliable.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: SMOG Index (1969, McLaughlin) stands for 'Simple Measure of Gobbledygook'. It was designed specifically for health literacy assessment and is considered more accurate than Flesch-Kincaid for texts above grade 4. It is the preferred formula for healthcare communications (required by many US health agencies). FORMULA: 1.0430 * sqrt(polysyllable_count * (30.0 / total_sentences)) + 3.1291. Where polysyllable_count = number of words with 3 or more syllables. MINIMUM REQUIREMENT: SMOG technically requires 30 sentences (10 from beginning, 10 from middle, 10 from end). For shorter texts, we use all sentences but add a confidence warning. If sentences < 3, return FormulaError::InsufficientText. INPUTS NEEDED: Tier 0 (sentence count) + Tier 1 (per-word syllable counts to identify polysyllabic words). IMPLEMENTATION: fn smog_index(stats: &TieredStats) -> Result<Score, FormulaError>. The polysyllable_count is pre-computed during Tier 1 stats collection (same pass that computes complex_words for Gunning Fog -- words with 3+ syllables). DESIGN NOTE: SMOG deliberately uses a square root, making it less sensitive to extreme values than linear formulas. It also rounds UP to the nearest grade, reflecting its conservative healthcare-oriented philosophy. We store the raw float and let interpretation handle rounding. CONFIDENCE: If sentences < 30, we set confidence = 'low' in the formula result metadata and emit a warning explaining the sample size issue.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .15 (polysyllable formulas). Gunning Fog and SMOG both count 3+ syllable words as their primary difficulty indicator. They share the same Tier 1 input (per-word syllable counts), the same polysyllable identification logic, and are natural companions. Implemented in the same module.

title: Implement Automated Readability Index formula
description: 4.71*(ARI_CHARS/W) + 0.5*(W/S) - 21.43. ARI chars = ASCII alphanumeric (includes digits, unlike letter-only counts). Policy is explicit and versioned. Tier 0 only.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Automated Readability Index (1967, Smith & Senter) was developed for the US military to automatically assess document readability using a typewriter (character counting was easier to automate than syllable counting in 1967). Like Coleman-Liau, it uses characters instead of syllables, making it fully deterministic and cheap to compute. FORMULA: 4.71 * (total_characters / total_words) + 0.5 * (total_words / total_sentences) - 21.43. Where total_characters = alphanumeric characters (letters + digits), excluding spaces and punctuation. INPUTS NEEDED: Tier 0 only (characters, words, sentences). No syllable counting required. IMPLEMENTATION: fn automated_readability_index(stats: &TieredStats) -> Result<Score, FormulaError>. FormulaError::InsufficientText if words < 1 or sentences < 1. DESIGN NOTE: ARI includes digits in its character count (unlike Coleman-Liau which counts only letters). This is intentional per the original paper -- technical documents with many numbers should score as harder to read. Our character counting must therefore be: chars where c.is_alphanumeric(). OUTPUT: US grade level, often with decimal (e.g., 7.3). The original paper maps to integer grades via ceiling, but we store raw float for consensus. Values 1-14+ typical; negative possible for very simple text. CONSENSUS ROLE: Together with Coleman-Liau, provides character-based grade estimates that complement syllable-based formulas. Since both use characters, if they strongly agree but syllable-based formulas disagree, it may indicate a syllable counting issue rather than a genuine readability disagreement.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .16 (Tier 0 formulas). Coleman-Liau and ARI use identical input tiers (Tier 0: characters/words/sentences only), are both 3-line pure functions, and belong together as the character-based formula pair. No reason to track them as separate work items.

title: Implement Tier 2 formulas: Dale-Chall + Spache
description: The two word-list-dependent formulas that need Tier 2 stats. Dale-Chall: 0.1579*DC_PDW + 0.0496*ASL; add 3.6365 if DC_PDW > 5%. Counts ALL occurrences of unfamiliar words. Returns both raw_unadjusted and adjusted score, plus grade_band and grade_midpoint. Spache: 0.121*ASL + 0.082*SP_UNFAM_PCT + 0.659. Counts UNIQUE unfamiliar words only (deduplicated per text). Warns when text appears above grade 4 (outside Spache's validated range). Both use the word lists from bead .11 (phf::Set lookups). Both check words lowercase, treat proper nouns as familiar, treat numbers as familiar.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Dale-Chall uses a word list (Tier 2) and produces a raw score that is NOT directly a grade level — it must be mapped to a grade band. This two-step output is important for the API. FORMULA: raw = 0.1579 * DC_PDW + 0.0496 * ASL. If DC_PDW > 5%, add 3.6365 to get the adjusted score. GRADE BAND MAPPING: 4.9-5.9 → grades 5-6, 6.0-6.9 → grades 7-8, 7.0-7.9 → grades 9-10, 8.0-8.9 → grades 11-12, 9.0-9.9 → grades 13-15 (college). Use the midpoint (e.g., 9.5 for 'grades 9-10') when feeding into consensus. OUTPUT: Return BOTH raw_unadjusted (before 3.6365 addition) and the adjusted score, plus grade_band string and grade_midpoint numeric. WHY BOTH: Transparency. Users can see the effect of the difficult-word threshold. A text with 4.9% difficult words (just below threshold) vs 5.1% (just above) will have a large score jump — exposing both values makes this visible.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Dale-Chall Readability Formula (1948, revised 1995) takes a fundamentally different approach from other formulas: instead of measuring word complexity via syllables or characters, it uses a curated list of ~3000 words that 80% of 4th graders can understand. Words NOT on this list are 'unfamiliar' and increase difficulty. This vocabulary-based approach often produces more accurate grade estimates for real-world text. FORMULA: 0.1579 * (difficult_words / total_words * 100.0) + 0.0496 * (total_words / total_sentences). If difficult_word_percentage > 5%: add 3.6365 to the raw score. Then convert raw score to grade range via lookup table. INPUTS NEEDED: Tier 0 (words, sentences) + Tier 2 (Dale-Chall word list lookup per word). This is the most expensive formula tier-wise. IMPLEMENTATION: fn dale_chall(stats: &TieredStats) -> Result<Score, FormulaError>. Requires iterating all words and checking each against the Dale-Chall familiar word set (from bead .11). Words are checked in lowercase. Proper nouns are considered familiar (they represent known entities, not vocabulary difficulty). Numbers are considered familiar. GRADE CONVERSION TABLE: raw < 5.0 -> grade 4, 5.0-5.9 -> grades 5-6, 6.0-6.9 -> grades 7-8, 7.0-7.9 -> grades 9-10, 8.0-8.9 -> grades 11-12, 9.0-9.9 -> grades 13-15, >= 10.0 -> grade 16+. We store both raw score and converted grade. PERFORMANCE: The phf::Set lookup is O(1), so the per-word check adds minimal overhead. The expensive part is the word iteration itself, which we already do for syllable counting.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .20 (Spache formula). Both formulas check words against phf::Set word lists from the same codegen module (.11), use similar difficulty-percentage calculations, and are the Tier 2 computation pair.

title: Implement input format auto-detection
description:
Detect HTML (,
, ), Markdown (--- frontmatter, # heading), or plain text. Applied in order per PRD §8.1. Must be fast (byte-level checks, no regex).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
CONTEXT: Auto-detection runs BEFORE preprocessing to select the right preprocessor. Must be byte-level fast (no regex). HEURISTICS (applied in order per PRD §8.1): (1) Starts with ,
, or

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Users submit text in various formats (plain text, HTML, Markdown) and should not need to specify which. Auto-detection determines the preprocessing pipeline to apply before tokenization. Getting this wrong means either mangled output (HTML tags counted as words) or unnecessary processing (running HTML stripper on plain text). GOAL: Detect input format with high confidence and route to the correct preprocessor chain. DETECTION HEURISTICS: (1) HTML: contains angle bracket followed by known tag name within first 1000 chars, OR starts with DOCTYPE or html tag (case-insensitive). (2) Markdown: contains markdown-specific patterns (# heading, [link](url), triple-backtick fence, blockquote at line start, * or - list items at line start) in first 2000 chars. (3) Plain text: default if neither HTML nor Markdown detected. IMPLEMENTATION: fn detect_format(input: &str) -> InputFormat where InputFormat is an enum { Html, Markdown, Plain }. Quick scan of first N chars only -- do NOT scan entire input for detection. EDGE CASES: Mixed content (HTML with Markdown inside) -> treat as HTML (HTML preprocessor runs first, then plain normalizer handles the rest). Plain text with angle brackets in prose (e.g., x is less than 5) should NOT trigger HTML detection -- require actual tag patterns. OVERRIDE: The API also accepts an explicit format hint in the request body that bypasses auto-detection. This is useful when callers know their input format.

title: Implement Spache Readability formula
description: 0.121*ASL + 0.082*SP_UNFAM_PCT + 0.659. Uses PERCENTAGE of unique unfamiliar words. Warn SPACHE_SCOPE when text is outside early-grade scope. Mark valid=false. Needs Tier 2 stats.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Spache Readability Formula (1953, revised 1974) is designed specifically for primary-grade materials (grades 1-3). It uses a word familiarity list (~1000 words known to early readers) similar to Dale-Chall but targeted at younger readers. Where Dale-Chall identifies 'difficult' words for 4th graders, Spache identifies 'unfamiliar' words for 1st-3rd graders. FORMULA: 0.121 * (total_words / total_sentences) + 0.082 * unfamiliar_word_count + 0.659. Where unfamiliar_word_count = count of unique words NOT on the Spache familiar list (each unfamiliar word counted only once regardless of frequency). INPUTS NEEDED: Tier 0 (words, sentences) + Tier 2 (Spache word list lookup per unique word). IMPLEMENTATION: fn spache(stats: &TieredStats) -> Result<Score, FormulaError>. Key difference from Dale-Chall: Spache counts UNIQUE unfamiliar words (deduplicated), not total occurrences. This requires collecting unique words first (HashSet), then checking each against the Spache list. DESIGN NOTE: The uniqueness requirement means a repeated difficult word only penalizes once. This reflects the formula's intended use: if a child encounters 'metamorphosis' 10 times in a chapter, they learn it from context after the first encounter. GRADE RANGE: Output is typically 1.0-4.0 (grades 1-4). Values above 4 suggest the text is beyond Spache's designed range, and we emit a warning recommending Dale-Chall instead. CONFIDENCE: If the text appears to be above grade 4 (Spache > 4.0 or other formulas suggest grade 5+), set confidence = 'low' with warning that Spache is not validated for this difficulty level.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .19 (Tier 2 formulas). Dale-Chall and Spache share identical dependency structure (both need word-list codegen from .11), identical lookup patterns (check word against phf::Set), and are implemented in adjacent files within formulas/. They are the word-list tier of computation and belong together as one deliverable.

title: Implement tiered stats computation
description: Tier 0 (always): bytes, words, sentences, letters, etc. Tier 1 (syllable-dep): syllables, polysyllables, complex_words. Tier 2 (wordlist-dep): DC/Spache difficult words. Only compute tiers needed by requested metrics.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Tiered computation is the key architectural optimization. Instead of computing ALL stats upfront, we only compute what the requested metrics actually need. TIERS: Tier 0 (always): bytes, words, sentences, paragraphs, letters, ari_characters, graphemes, unique_words, hapax_count, longest_sentence_words, averages. Tier 1 (syllable-dependent): syllables, polysyllables, complex_words, avg_syllables_per_word. Needed by: Flesch, Flesch-Kincaid, Gunning Fog, SMOG. Tier 2 (word-list-dependent): dale_chall_difficult_words/percentage, spache_unique_unfamiliar_words/percentage. Needed by: Dale-Chall, Spache. EXAMPLE SAVINGS: metrics:[coleman_liau, ari] → Tier 0 ONLY. No syllable counting (expensive), no word-list lookups. Should be ≥30% faster than metrics:[all]. IMPLEMENTATION: The analyze() function checks which tiers are needed based on the metrics array, then conditionally calls compute_tier_1() and compute_tier_2(). TextStats uses Option<usize> for Tier 1/2 fields — None means not yet computed.

title: Implement consensus grade algorithm
description: Pinned math: 5+ metrics → drop min/max, mean of rest. 3-4 → median. 2 → mean. 1 → use directly (low confidence). 0 → null + NO_VALID_METRICS. Exclude invalid metrics. Stability thresholds: high ≤2, medium ≤5, low >5.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The consensus algorithm takes all grade-level formula outputs and produces a single 'primary' grade. This is the main product answer — the one number clients display to users. PINNED ALGORITHM (no ambiguity): (1) Collect valid grade-level metrics: FK, Fog, CL, SMOG, ARI, Dale-Chall midpoint. Add Spache only if early-grade. (2) Exclude any with valid=false or error. (3) Sort ascending. (4) Aggregate by count: 5+ → drop min/max, arithmetic mean of rest. 3-4 → median (or avg of middle two if even). 2 → arithmetic mean. 1 → use directly, confidence=low. 0 → null, emit NO_VALID_METRICS. (5) Compute agreement: min_grade, max_grade, spread=max-min, stability (high ≤2.0, medium ≤5.0, low >5.0). (6) Confidence: high = ≥300 words + ≥10 sentences + ≥5 valid + stability=high. Medium = ≥100 words + ≥3 sentences + ≥3 valid + stability≤medium. Low = everything else. WHY PINNED: Vague algorithms ('trimmed median-style') can't be golden-tested. Exact steps enable deterministic assertions.

title: Implement paragraph breakdown and hardest-sentences extraction
description: Per-paragraph: word count, sentence count, avg_sentence_length, avg_syllables_per_word, difficulty rating (easy/moderate/hard). Plus: rank sentences by composite difficulty (word count + syllables/word + complex word density), return top N (max 10) with 80-char preview. Paragraph boundaries from preprocessing; sentence stats from tokenizer. Most actionable output for content editors — identifies both which paragraphs are hardest and which specific sentences to rewrite.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Paragraph-level breakdown is one of the most actionable outputs for content editors. They want to know WHICH paragraph to rewrite, not just a whole-document score. IMPLEMENTATION: During preprocessing, paragraph boundaries are already detected (double newlines in plain text, block elements in HTML, blank lines in Markdown). Store sentence indices per paragraph. Per-paragraph stats: word_count, sentence_count, avg_sentence_length, avg_syllables_per_word (if Tier 1 computed). Difficulty rating: 'easy' (ASL < 15), 'moderate' (15-25), 'hard' (>25). COST: Near-zero incremental cost — we already know paragraph boundaries from preprocessing. Just group the per-sentence stats we already have. GATE: Behind options.include_paragraphs=false (default) so it doesn't bloat the default response. When enabled, adds the 'paragraphs' array to the response.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .24 (hardest-sentences extraction). Paragraph breakdown and sentence ranking operate on the same per-sentence stats, share the same module, and produce complementary actionable output.

title: Implement hardest-sentences extraction
description: Rank sentences by composite difficulty (word count + syllables_per_word + complex_word_count). Return top N (max 10). Include 80-char preview. Most actionable output for content editors.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: After paragraph breakdown, the natural next question is 'which specific SENTENCES should I rewrite?' This extraction identifies the N hardest sentences for targeted editing. RANKING: Composite difficulty score per sentence = (word_count * 0.4) + (syllables_per_word * 0.3) + (complex_word_pct * 0.3). Normalized to the document's own distribution. Return top N (configurable 1-10, default 0 = disabled). OUTPUT: index, paragraph_index, words, syllables_per_word, complex_word_count, preview (first 80 chars). PRIVACY: The 'preview' field shows sentence beginnings. If the client wants zero text output, they can set include_hardest_sentences=0. The 80-char cap prevents leaking full sentences in logs/errors. CONSIDERATION: This requires Tier 1 stats (syllables). If only Tier 0 metrics are requested AND hardest_sentences > 0, we need to compute Tier 1 anyway. Document this interaction.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .23 (paragraph breakdown). Hardest-sentences extraction operates on the same per-sentence stats and belongs in the same module. Single bead now covers both paragraph difficulty and sentence ranking.

title: Implement warnings and confidence engine
description: Evaluate all warning conditions (SHORT_TEXT, FEW_SENTENCES, SMOG_SHORT_SAMPLE, SPACHE_SCOPE, SYLLABLE_ESTIMATE, etc.). Compute confidence level (high/medium/low) per PRD §7.2 rules.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The warnings engine evaluates all conditions after stats and formulas are computed, producing a list of Warning objects with code, message, and severity. IMPLEMENTATION: Check each condition from the PRD §15 warning table. Multiple warnings can fire simultaneously (e.g., SHORT_TEXT + FEW_SENTENCES + SMOG_SHORT_SAMPLE for a 2-sentence input). Warnings cascade into formula validity (SMOG_SHORT_SAMPLE → smog.valid=false) and confidence level (SHORT_TEXT → reduces confidence). Severity levels: 'error' (analysis may be meaningless — EMPTY_TEXT, NO_VALID_METRICS), 'warning' (specific metrics affected — FEW_SENTENCES, SMOG_SHORT_SAMPLE), 'info' (informational only — SYLLABLE_ESTIMATE, NORMALIZED_SCORE). Both top-level warnings and per-formula warnings exist. Top-level: document-wide conditions. Per-formula: metric-specific conditions.

title: Wire up analyze() and analyze_batch() top-level API
description: The public entry points that orchestrate: preprocess → tokenize → compute tiers → run formulas → consensus → paragraphs → hardest sentences → warnings → assemble AnalysisResult.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: This is the integration layer that wires everything together. It's the crate's public face — the only thing the Worker and CLI call. FLOW: (1) Determine input format (auto-detect or explicit). (2) Preprocess (plain/html/markdown). (3) Tokenize: sentence segmentation → word tokenization. (4) Compute Tier 0 stats (always). (5) Determine which Tiers 1/2 are needed from requested metrics + options. (6) If needed: compute Tier 1 (syllable counting with LRU cache). (7) If needed: compute Tier 2 (word-list lookups). (8) Run each requested formula, collect Results. (9) Run consensus algorithm over valid grade-level results. (10) If include_paragraphs: compute paragraph breakdown. (11) If include_hardest_sentences > 0: extract hardest sentences. (12) Evaluate all warning conditions. (13) Assemble AnalysisResult. ERROR ISOLATION: Each step that can fail returns Result. A formula failure is captured in the result (valid=false), not propagated as an error that kills the whole analysis. Only truly fatal errors (e.g., no input text at all) return Err from analyze().

title: Implement plain-text preprocessor
description: Normalize Unicode quotes/dashes, preserve paragraph boundaries (double newlines), collapse whitespace, preserve sentence-ending punctuation, strip control chars.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Plain text is the simplest input path but still needs normalization for consistent formula computation. Unicode text in the wild contains typographic quotes (U+2018-201D), em/en dashes (U+2013-2014), non-breaking spaces (U+00A0), and other variants that would confuse sentence/word boundary detection. GOAL: Produce a clean UTF-8 string where: (1) paragraph boundaries (double newlines) are preserved (critical for paragraph-level breakdown feature), (2) fancy punctuation is normalized to ASCII equivalents for reliable regex matching, (3) runs of whitespace collapse to single spaces within paragraphs, (4) control characters (U+0000-001F except newline) are stripped, (5) sentence-ending punctuation (.!?) is preserved exactly. IMPLEMENTATION: Single-pass char iterator with state machine: emit normalized char or skip. No allocations beyond the output String (pre-allocated to input.len()). ORDER MATTERS: This runs AFTER format detection but BEFORE sentence segmentation. Must be idempotent. EDGE CASES: BOM (U+FEFF) at start -> strip. Ellipsis (U+2026) -> three periods (counts as sentence boundary). Soft hyphen (U+00AD) -> strip entirely.

title: Implement HTML preprocessor
description: Strip script/style/noscript/comments, convert block elements to paragraph/sentence boundaries, decode HTML entities, strip remaining tags. No heavy DOM dep. Emit HTML_MALFORMED warning if uncertain.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: HTML is the most common real-world input format (scraped web pages, CMS output, email bodies). We must extract readable prose while discarding navigation, scripts, styles, and structural markup. GOAL: Strip HTML to plain text while preserving semantic structure: (1) block elements (p, div, h1-h6, li, blockquote) become paragraph boundaries, (2) inline elements (span, em, strong, a) are transparent, (3) script/style/noscript/template contents are fully removed, (4) br becomes newline, (5) HTML entities decoded (both named and numeric), (6) multiple consecutive whitespace collapsed post-strip. IMPLEMENTATION: Lightweight state-machine parser (NOT a full DOM). Walk chars tracking: in_tag, in_script, in_style, entity_buf. No dependency on heavy HTML parsers (scraper/html5ever would add 200KB+ to WASM). Tag detection is case-insensitive. Self-closing tags handled. Malformed HTML (unclosed tags) handled gracefully via state reset heuristics. OUTPUT: Feeds into plain-text preprocessor for final normalization. PERFORMANCE: Single-pass O(n), no tree construction, no allocations beyond output buffer. TESTING: Golden tests with real-world HTML snippets (Wikipedia article, Medium post, marketing email) comparing extracted text against hand-verified expected output.

title: Implement Markdown-lite preprocessor
description: Strip frontmatter, treat headings/list items as sentences, ignore fenced code blocks, strip link URLs (preserve text), strip images, handle table cells. Lightweight — not a full parser.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Markdown is common in developer tools, documentation platforms, and CMS systems (Ghost, Notion exports, GitHub READMEs). Unlike HTML, we only need to handle a subset since full CommonMark parsing would be overkill for readability analysis. GOAL: Strip Markdown formatting to plain text while preserving prose structure: (1) headings (# through ######) become paragraph boundaries, (2) emphasis markers (*/_) stripped, (3) links [text](url) -> text only, (4) images ![alt](src) -> alt text only, (5) code blocks (fenced and indented) REMOVED entirely (code is not prose), (6) inline code backticks stripped, (7) blockquotes (>) -> text without marker, (8) lists (- * + 1.) -> text without markers, (9) horizontal rules -> paragraph boundary, (10) HTML within markdown -> pass through HTML preprocessor. IMPLEMENTATION: Line-by-line processing since most Markdown constructs are line-oriented. Regex-lite patterns for inline formatting. Code fence state tracking (triple backtick open/close). ORDERING: Runs BEFORE HTML preprocessor (handles embedded HTML), then feeds into plain-text normalizer. NOT A FULL PARSER: We intentionally skip reference links, footnotes, tables (cell text extracted but structure lost), and other extensions. This is 'Markdown-lite' -- good enough for prose extraction, not a spec-compliant renderer.

title: Implement sentence segmentation
description: Split on .?! with abbreviation handling (Mr., Dr., U.S., e.g., etc.), decimal protection (3.14), acronym protection. Fallback: treat headings/list items as sentences. Emit NO_SENTENCE_TERMINATOR warning.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Sentence segmentation is the most error-prone part of readability scoring. Over-splitting (treating Mr. as sentence end) deflates ASL and makes text appear easier than it is. Under-splitting (missing a period) inflates ASL. RULES: Split on .?! and repeated terminal punctuation (!!!, ???). Do NOT split: abbreviations (Mr., Mrs., Ms., Dr., Prof., Sr., Jr., St., vs., etc., e.g., i.e., Inc., Ltd., a.m., p.m.), initials/acronyms (U.S., U.K., A.I., F. Scott Fitzgerald), decimals (3.14, 1,000.50). Treat headings/list items as sentence-like units when no terminal punctuation. Guarantee ≥1 sentence for any non-empty input. Emit NO_SENTENCE_TERMINATOR warning when using fallback. IMPLEMENTATION: Use a state machine or rule-based splitter with an abbreviation lookup set. Consider the heuristic: a period followed by a lowercase letter is NOT a sentence boundary.

title: Implement word tokenization
description: Lexical tokens with ≥1 letter. Contractions = 1 word. Hyphenated compounds = 1 word (internally split for Fog). Pure numbers excluded from syllables but included in ARI chars. Normalize curly apostrophes.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Word tokenization defines what 'a word' is for ALL formulas. This is the foundational unit — get it wrong and every score is wrong. RULES: A word = lexical token with ≥1 letter. Contractions (don't, can't, we're) = 1 word. Hyphenated compounds (well-known) = 1 word for counting but internally splittable for Gunning Fog complex-word exclusion. Pure numbers (42, 3.14) are NOT words (no syllables) but their characters count for ARI. Preserve original casing for proper-noun heuristics. Normalize to lowercase for dictionary lookup. Curly apostrophes (U+2019) → ASCII apostrophe. ARENA ALLOCATION: Use bumpalo for per-request word slice storage to minimize WASM alloc overhead. The tokenizer produces many small allocations that live for exactly one request — perfect arena use case.

title: Implement syllable counter (rules + exception dictionary)
description: Two-layer syllable counting: (1) Exception dictionary — phf::Map of 500-2000 irregular words (business=2, beautiful=3, colonel=2, etc.) compiled via build.rs from data/syllable_exceptions.tsv. (2) Rule-based fallback — vowel group counting with silent-e, terminal -le, diphthong, -ed/-es/-ing adjustments. Always returns max(1, count). Combined accuracy target: 95%+ on common English. Lookup order: cache -> exceptions -> rules. Track counts from each source for transparency (syllable_estimates_from_dictionary vs from_rules in stats).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The rule-based syllable estimator is the fallback for all words not in the exception dictionary or CMU subset. It handles ~80-95% of words in typical text. ALGORITHM: (1) Count vowel groups (a,e,i,o,u,y) as syllable nuclei. (2) Subtract 1 for silent-e at word end (but not -le). (3) Add 1 for terminal -le preceded by a consonant (ta-ble, lit-tle). (4) Handle -ed: silent after t/d (want-ed=2), silent elsewhere (walk-ed=1, but need-ed=2). (5) Handle -es: usually silent (makes=1) but adds syllable after sibilants (class-es=2). (6) Handle -ing: not a separate syllable if preceded by vowel+consonant pattern already counted. (7) Diphthongs (oi, ou, oo, ea, etc.) count as 1 nucleus, not 2. (8) Always return max(1, count). ACCURACY: Rule-based is ~85-90% accurate on English. The exception dictionary and optional CMU feature exist to cover the remaining irregular words. Track syllable_estimates_from_rules vs from_dictionary in stats for transparency.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .9 (syllable exception dictionary). Rules and exceptions are two layers of one syllable-counting function — they share the same lookup order, same module, same test fixtures.

title: Build syllable exception dictionary
description: Hardcoded map of irregular words where rules fail: business(2), queue(1), people(2), beautiful(3), colonel(2), Wednesday(2), coyote(3), recipe(3), etc. Loaded from data/syllable_exceptions.tsv.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: English syllable counting is notoriously irregular. Rule-based estimation (bead .8) gets ~85-90% accuracy, but common words with irregular syllable patterns need explicit overrides. The exception dictionary provides ground-truth counts for high-frequency irregular words. GOAL: Ship a curated dictionary of 500-2000 words where rule-based counting is known to be wrong. Format: word -> syllable_count. Examples: 'every' (2 not 3), 'poem' (2 not 1), 'beautiful' (3 not 4), 'business' (2 not 3), 'chocolate' (3 not 4), 'comfortable' (3 not 5), 'different' (2 not 3), 'evening' (2 not 3). IMPLEMENTATION: Compile into a phf::Map<&str, u8> via build.rs for O(1) lookup with zero runtime allocation. Source file: wordlists/syllable_exceptions.txt (one 'word N' pair per line). LOOKUP ORDER: (1) check syllable LRU cache, (2) check exception dictionary, (3) fall back to rule-based estimator. This ordering means the cache stores final answers (whether from exceptions or rules), maximizing cache hit value. MAINTENANCE: The exception list should be expanded based on syllable-tests accuracy measurements (Phase 4 bead .6). Any word where our rules differ from CMU Pronouncing Dictionary ground truth by 2+ syllables is a candidate.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .8 (syllable counter). The exception dictionary and rule-based estimator are two layers of the same syllable-counting module. They share the same source file (syllables/mod.rs), same build.rs integration, same test fixtures, and same public API (fn count_syllables). Separating them into two beads suggested they could be implemented independently, but the exception dict IS the first thing the estimator checks — they are inseparable.

title: Phase 3: Worker Wrapper
description: Implement the readability-worker crate: HTTP routing, csvkey auth, size limits, ETag caching, response format selection, CORS, error handling, panic recovery, and Cloudflare deployment config.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: PHASE OVERVIEW: The Worker phase wraps the readability-core engine in a Cloudflare Worker HTTP interface. This crate (readability-worker) is intentionally thin -- it contains NO business logic, only HTTP plumbing: request parsing, csvkey authentication, response serialization, error formatting, CORS, logging, and panic recovery. The strict separation means we can swap the HTTP layer without touching computation logic, and we can test computation without WASM. KEY CONCERNS: (1) WASM compilation target (wasm32-unknown-unknown) restricts available APIs -- no std::time, no filesystem, no threads. (2) CF Worker CPU budget (30ms paid plan) requires efficient request handling -- no unnecessary copies or allocations. (3) csvkey authentication follows the rusty-gateway pattern (constant-time comparison, Cloudflare secret binding). (4) ETag caching is deterministic (SHA-256 of input text) enabling 304 Not Modified responses for repeated analyses. (5) Panic recovery at the Worker boundary catches any unwind from readability-core and returns a structured 500 error instead of crashing the isolate. (6) Batch endpoint provides per-text error isolation -- one bad text does not fail the entire batch. DEPENDENCIES: Requires Phase 2 completion (readability-core must exist to call). Phase 1 types crate provides shared request/response types.

title: Scaffold readability-worker crate
description: Create worker crate with worker dep, wasm-bindgen, console_error_panic_hook. Set up #[event(fetch)] entry point. Depend on readability-core + readability-types.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: readability-worker is the Cloudflare Worker crate that bridges HTTP requests to the pure-Rust readability-core engine. It depends on worker (CF Rust SDK), readability-core, and readability-types. This crate handles all platform-specific concerns: HTTP parsing, routing, authentication, serialization, error responses, and panic recovery. GOAL: Create the crate with proper Cargo.toml (worker, worker-macros, serde, serde_json, readability-core, readability-types), src/lib.rs with the worker main macro, and module structure: routes/, auth.rs, error.rs, response.rs, middleware.rs. ARCHITECTURE: The worker crate is deliberately thin -- it should contain zero business logic. Its job is: (1) parse HTTP request, (2) authenticate via csvkey, (3) deserialize request body, (4) call readability_core::analyze() or analyze_batch(), (5) serialize response, (6) handle errors/panics. All readability computation stays in readability-core for testability. WASM TARGET: This crate compiles to wasm32-unknown-unknown only. It cannot use std::time, std::fs, or anything that requires OS syscalls. The worker SDK provides CF-specific alternatives (Date::now() for timing). BUILD: Uses worker-build which invokes wasm-pack under the hood. Optimized with wasm-opt (included in CF build pipeline). SIZE BUDGET: Target under 1MB compressed WASM for fast cold starts.

title: Implement panic recovery boundary
description: Wrap analyze() in catch_unwind at the Worker boundary. On panic: return 500 INTERNAL_PANIC, log byte_length + text_hash + sanitized panic message. Ensures one bad input doesn't crash the isolate permanently.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: WASM panics in Workers abort the isolate. If a panic escapes from readability-core, the Worker dies and Cloudflare must spin up a new isolate for subsequent requests. This is expensive and disrupts the LRU syllable cache. IMPLEMENTATION: Wrap the analyze()/analyze_batch() call at the Worker level in std::panic::catch_unwind(). This requires the core function to be UnwindSafe (no &mut references across the boundary — use owned types). On panic: (1) Return HTTP 500 with {error: {code: 'INTERNAL_PANIC', message: 'Internal error', request_id: '...'}}. (2) Log: byte_length, text_hash, sanitized panic message (strip any substrings matching user text). (3) NEVER include user text in the panic log. WHY NOT JUST 'NO PANICS': Defense in depth. We write panic-free code (no unwrap on user-derived data), but bugs happen. catch_unwind is the safety net that prevents one bad request from killing the isolate and its warm syllable cache.

title: Implement batch analysis endpoint
description: POST /v1/batch accepts {texts: [{id, text},...], options}. Max 10 texts. Per-text error isolation (one failure doesn't affect others). Shared options. Returns {results: [{id, status, data/error}]}.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Batch endpoint serves the primary audience (SEO/content-quality tools) that always process multiple pages. Without batch, every integration builds its own retry/fan-out logic. IMPLEMENTATION: POST /v1/batch accepts {texts: [{id, text}, ...], options}. Max 10 texts per request. Total body max 1MB. Shared options apply to all texts. Internally: iterate texts, call analyze() for each, collect results. Per-text error isolation: if text[3] is empty, its result has status:'error' — other texts still return successfully. RESPONSE: {schema_version, request_id, results: [{id, status:'ok'|'error', data: AnalysisResult | null, error: {code, message} | null}]}. WHY MAX 10: CPU budget. 10 texts * 5ms each = 50ms worst case. Cloudflare Workers have a 30-50ms CPU time limit on free tier. 10 texts keeps us within budget even for moderate-length inputs. Paid plans can configure higher limits. CONSIDERATION: The CPU budget (30ms) applies to the entire batch, not per-text. If text 8 of 10 exceeds budget, texts 9-10 get TIMEOUT results.

title: Lock Cloudflare config decisions and write wrangler.toml
description: PRODUCT DECISIONS: 128KB single max, 1MB batch max, 10 texts/batch, 30ms CPU budget, worker name 'rusty-readability'. These are stable API contracts. WRANGLER.TOML: name, main=build/worker/shim.mjs, compatibility_date, build command (rustup + wasm32 + worker-build --release, mirrors rusty-gateway). Vars: READABILITY_VERSION, MAX_BYTES=131072, BATCH_MAX_BYTES=1048576, BATCH_MAX_TEXTS=10, LOG_LEVEL=info. Secret: CSVKEY. Environments: staging. This bead combines the decision-making (what values) with the implementation (write the file) since they happen in the same sitting.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: wrangler.toml configures the Cloudflare Worker deployment -- name, routes, build commands, environment variables, secrets, and compatibility settings. Getting this right is essential for both local development (wrangler dev) and production deployment. GOAL: Create a wrangler.toml that mirrors the proven pattern from rusty-gateway with adjustments for this project. KEY SETTINGS: name = "rusty-readability". main = "build/worker/shim.mjs". compatibility_date = current date. SECRETS: CSVKEY (bound at runtime via wrangler secret put). BUILD COMMAND: Same as rusty-gateway: curl rustup, install stable, add wasm32-unknown-unknown target, install worker-build, run worker-build --release. VARS: None needed (all config is via secrets or hardcoded). LIMITS: CF Workers Free plan: 10ms CPU time, 128MB memory. CF Workers Paid: 50ms CPU (or 30ms with comfortable margin). We target paid plan. COMPATIBILITY FLAGS: nodejs_compat (needed by worker shim). SIZE: wasm-opt runs during worker-build --release, minimizing binary size. ENVIRONMENTS: [env.staging] with separate name for pre-production testing. ROUTES: Configured per-domain later (not in initial wrangler.toml -- use workers.dev subdomain during development). LOCAL DEV: wrangler dev runs locally with --var CSVKEY:test-key for development authentication.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former Phase 1 bead .7 (lock CF config). The product decisions (128KB max, 30ms budget, etc.) and the file that encodes them (wrangler.toml) are inseparable — you write the file when you make the decisions.

title: Implement csvkey query param authentication
description: Extract csvkey from URL query params. Validate against CSVKEY Worker secret using constant-time comparison. Return 401 JSON error on failure. Skip auth for /health and OPTIONS. Mirrors rusty-gateway pattern exactly.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Authentication uses the exact same pattern as rusty-gateway: a CSVKEY secret stored in Cloudflare, passed as a ?csvkey= query parameter, validated with constant-time byte comparison. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Parse URL query params into HashMap. (2) Extract 'csvkey' value. (3) Load CSVKEY from env.secret('CSVKEY'). (4) Constant-time comparison: fold XOR over zipped bytes, check == 0. Length mismatch = immediate false. (5) Return 401 JSON error on failure. SKIP AUTH FOR: GET /health (liveness probe), OPTIONS /* (CORS preflight — browsers can't add query params to preflight). WHY CONSTANT-TIME: Prevents timing attacks where an attacker can determine how many bytes of the key are correct by measuring response latency. Even though this is unlikely in practice for a readability API, it's trivial to implement correctly and mirrors rusty-gateway. WHY NOT HEADER-BASED: Query param is simpler for curl/testing, matches the existing rusty-gateway pattern, and doesn't require clients to set custom headers (which some tools make awkward).

title: Implement route handlers
description: POST /v1/analyze, POST /v1/batch, GET /v1/metrics, GET /v1/version, GET /health, OPTIONS /*. Method validation (405 on wrong method). Path matching.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The Worker exposes 4 routes: POST /analyze (single text), POST /analyze/batch (multiple texts), GET /health (liveness check), and a catch-all 404. Route matching must be fast and allocation-free. GOAL: Implement a simple pattern matcher that dispatches requests to the correct handler based on method + path. No need for a full routing framework -- we have exactly 4 patterns. IMPLEMENTATION: Match on (req.method(), req.path().as_str()) tuple. POST /analyze -> handle_analyze(). POST /analyze/batch -> handle_batch(). GET /health -> handle_health(). _ -> json_error(404, "Not Found"). DESIGN DECISIONS: (1) No path parameters -- all endpoints are fixed strings. (2) No query string routing -- query params are for auth (csvkey) and options (format), handled within handlers. (3) Trailing slash tolerance: /analyze/ should work same as /analyze. (4) Method matching is exact -- GET /analyze returns 405 Method Not Allowed (not 404). This helps API consumers debug integration issues. CORS: Preflight OPTIONS requests must be handled before route matching (see cors bead .7). The route handler only sees non-preflight requests. HANDLER SIGNATURES: Each handler receives the parsed/authenticated request context and returns Result of Response or worker Error. Handlers are async (required by CF Worker SDK) but our computation is synchronous within them.

title: Implement request size limit enforcement
description: Check Content-Length BEFORE reading body. 128KB single, 1MB batch. Reject with 413 immediately. If chunked (no Content-Length), read up to limit+1 and reject if exceeded. Never allocate oversized strings.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Size limit enforcement MUST happen before JSON parsing. If we parse first, a 100MB payload will be fully allocated as a String before we can reject it — wasting CPU and memory that could crash the isolate. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Check Content-Length header. If present and > limit, return 413 immediately (zero body bytes read). (2) If Content-Length absent (chunked encoding), read the stream up to limit+1 bytes. If we read limit+1 bytes, reject with 413. (3) Only AFTER size validation: parse JSON body. LIMITS: Single request = 128KB (READABILITY_MAX_BYTES var). Batch request = 1MB (READABILITY_BATCH_MAX_BYTES var). Configurable per deployment. WHY PRODUCT LIMITS < PLATFORM LIMITS: Cloudflare allows much larger bodies, but readability analysis is CPU-bound. A 10MB text would consume the entire Worker CPU quota. Product-specific limits keep response times predictable and prevent accidental misuse.

title: Implement ETag caching
description: Compute ETag from sha256(text) + sha256(options). Return ETag header on success. Check If-None-Match and return 304 before running analysis. Major perf win for edit-recheck workflows.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: ETag caching enables 304 Not Modified responses for repeated submissions of the same text+options. This is a massive win for the 'edit, re-check, edit, re-check' workflow common in content editing tools. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) After size validation but BEFORE full analysis, compute ETag = sha256(text) + sha256(serialized_options). (2) Check If-None-Match request header against computed ETag. (3) If match → return 304 immediately (no analysis runs). (4) If no match → run analysis, return response with ETag header. FORMAT: ETag: "v1-<hex16_text>-<hex8_opts>" (truncated hashes for header size). CONSIDERATION: ETag computation requires hashing the full text body, which means we still need to read and allocate the body. But we skip the expensive analysis pipeline. For a 5000-word text, analysis is ~10ms; hashing is ~0.1ms. The 100x speedup on cache hit justifies the hash cost. BATCH: ETag for batch requests uses hash of all texts + options combined.

title: Implement response format selection
