description: full (default, everything), compact (scores+stats+warnings, no explanations/engine metadata), scores_only (minimal ~200 bytes: primary + flat scores + hash). Driven by options.response_format.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Three response formats serve different use cases at different payload sizes. Full (~3KB+) for detailed analysis, compact (~1.5KB) for moderate integrations, scores_only (~200 bytes) for high-volume SEO crawlers. IMPLEMENTATION: A single analyze() call produces the full AnalysisResult internally. The response_format option controls which fields are serialized. Use #[serde(skip_serializing_if)] or manual serialization selection. FULL: Everything — engine metadata, input metadata, stats, full score objects with interpretations/warnings, primary with agreement/drivers/summary, paragraphs (if requested), hardest_sentences (if requested), top-level warnings. COMPACT: scores (but flat — raw values only, no per-formula warnings/interpretation), stats, primary (score/label/confidence only), top-level warnings. No engine metadata, no explanations. SCORES_ONLY: {primary: {score, label, confidence}, scores: {metric: value, ...}, text_hash}. One flat object, ~200 bytes. WHY: An SEO tool analyzing 10,000 pages doesn't need 3KB * 10,000 = 30MB of metadata. scores_only at 200 bytes * 10,000 = 2MB is practical.

title: Implement CORS preflight and headers
description: OPTIONS returns 204 with Access-Control-Allow-Origin/Methods/Headers. All responses get CORS headers. No auth required for OPTIONS. Origin allowlist via READABILITY_ALLOWED_ORIGINS var (default *).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) headers are needed if the API will be called from browser-based applications (JavaScript fetch from a different domain). Even if initial use is server-to-server, adding CORS from the start prevents painful debugging later when someone inevitably tries to call it from a frontend. GOAL: Handle CORS preflight (OPTIONS) requests and add appropriate headers to all responses. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) OPTIONS requests: return 204 No Content with headers: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * (or configurable origin), Access-Control-Allow-Methods: POST, GET, OPTIONS, Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type, Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400. (2) All other responses: add Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. DESIGN DECISIONS: (1) Allow-Origin: * for now (public API behind csvkey auth). Can be restricted later via environment variable if needed. (2) Max-Age: 86400 (24h) so browsers cache preflight results and do not re-send OPTIONS for every request. (3) We do NOT expose custom response headers via Access-Control-Expose-Headers unless specifically needed. (4) CORS is checked BEFORE auth -- an OPTIONS preflight should succeed without csvkey (browsers cannot add custom query params to preflight). PLACEMENT: CORS handling wraps the entire request pipeline. In code: check if OPTIONS -> return preflight response. Otherwise, proceed to auth -> route -> handler, then add CORS headers to the final response before returning.

title: Implement structured error responses
description: JSON error shape: {error: {code, message, request_id}}. Map AnalyzeError → HTTP status (400/413/415/422/500). Never include user text in errors. Always include request_id. Never serialize NaN/Infinity.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Consistent, structured error responses make API integration significantly easier. Every error -- whether auth failure, bad input, internal panic, or timeout -- should return the same JSON shape so clients can parse errors uniformly. GOAL: Define a standard error response format and implement helper functions to generate error responses throughout the worker. ERROR RESPONSE SHAPE: { "error": { "code": "string_code", "message": "Human-readable description", "status": 400 } }. The string_code is machine-parseable (e.g., "unauthorized", "invalid_input", "text_too_large", "internal_error", "rate_limited"). IMPLEMENTATION: fn json_error(status: u16, message: &str, code: &str) -> Response. Maps to HTTP status codes: 400 (bad request/validation), 401 (auth), 405 (method not allowed), 413 (text too large), 429 (rate limited), 500 (internal error), 504 (CPU timeout). DESIGN DECISIONS: (1) Always JSON, never plain text errors -- even for 404. (2) Error codes are stable API contract -- once shipped, they do not change. (3) Internal details (stack traces, memory addresses) NEVER appear in error responses (security: information disclosure). (4) Content-Type: application/json on all error responses. (5) Errors include ETag: none (do not cache error responses). INTEGRATION: Every handler and middleware uses json_error(). The panic recovery boundary (bead .10) catches panics and converts them to json_error(500, "Internal error", "internal_error") without leaking panic payload.

title: Implement privacy-safe structured logging and observability headers
description: console_log! structured JSON per request: request_id, path, status, duration_ms, bytes, words, sentences, metrics, response_format, etag_hit, warnings, cache_hit_rate, cpu_budget_pct. NEVER log: text, csvkey, snippets, tokens. PLUS: opt-in diagnostic response headers (via ?verbose=true): X-Processing-Time-Ms, X-Word-Count, X-Formulas-Computed, X-Cache-Hit-Rate. These enable client-side monitoring and CF Logpush/Analytics Engine integration. Parseable by Cloudflare Logpush.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Observability in a Cloudflare Worker is limited compared to traditional servers. There is no persistent filesystem, no log aggregation daemon, and console.log() output goes to wrangler tail or the CF dashboard. Structured logging (JSON lines) enables filtering and analysis in CF's log pipeline or any external drain. GOAL: Implement structured request/response logging that captures timing, input characteristics, and outcome without logging any user text content (privacy requirement). LOG FIELDS PER REQUEST: { timestamp, request_id (from CF headers), method, path, input_format, text_length_bytes, word_count, sentence_count, formulas_computed, consensus_grade, response_status, duration_ms, cache_hit (ETag 304), error_code (if any) }. PRIVACY RULES: (1) NEVER log request body text content. (2) NEVER log individual scores (could fingerprint content). (3) Log only aggregate stats (counts, timing) and metadata. (4) csvkey value NEVER appears in logs. IMPLEMENTATION: Use console_log! macro (available in CF Worker Rust SDK) with JSON-formatted strings. One log line per request (not scattered throughout processing). Log at end of request after response is ready but before returning. LEVELS: Info for successful requests, Warn for client errors (4xx), Error for server errors (5xx). PERFORMANCE: Logging adds less than 0.1ms overhead (just string formatting). No external calls. FUTURE: If CF log drain is configured, these structured logs can be shipped to Datadog/Grafana/etc for dashboarding.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former Phase 5 bead .4 (structured observability). The diagnostic response headers (X-Processing-Time-Ms etc.) are trivially added in the same logging code path. One coherent instrumentation concern.

title: Phase 4: CLI and Validation Harness
description: Build the readability-cli tool and full test suite: golden tests, snapshot tests, cross-library comparisons, fuzz/property tests, mutation tests, and worker integration tests.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: PHASE OVERVIEW: Validation ensures correctness, accuracy, and reliability before deployment. This phase includes multiple testing strategies: golden tests (exact expected output for hand-counted fixtures), snapshot tests (insta crate for detecting unexpected changes), cross-library comparison (validating against textstat/readability-scores), tokenizer edge-case tests, syllable accuracy measurement against CMU dictionary, Worker integration tests (full HTTP cycle), and a CLI tool for manual exploration. WHY MULTIPLE STRATEGIES: No single testing approach catches all bugs. Golden tests catch formula regressions but require manual maintenance. Snapshots catch unexpected changes but do not verify correctness. Cross-library comparison validates against consensus but other tools may have bugs too. Tokenizer tests catch segmentation edge cases. Syllable accuracy tests measure systematic bias. Integration tests catch serialization/auth/routing issues. Together they provide high confidence. DEPENDENCY: Requires Phase 2 (engine) and Phase 3 (worker) to be complete. The CLI tool (bead .1) can begin once readability-core exists (Phase 2), independent of the Worker. CADENCE: Tests should be written alongside implementation (not as an afterthought), but this phase is sequenced last because it validates the complete system. Individual unit tests within Phases 2-3 are expected but not tracked as separate beads.

title: Build readability-cli tool
description: Subcommands: analyze <file>, corpus-report <dir>, golden-update, syllable-bench. Supports --format json/csv/table. stdin piping. Used in CI for golden test management and cross-library comparison automation.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The CLI is a FIRST-CLASS developer tool (not optional). It's how you test the engine locally, generate golden test baselines, run corpus reports, and debug syllable accuracy. Without it, every developer interaction requires spinning up the full Worker. SUBCOMMANDS: (1) analyze <file|-> [--format json|csv|table] [--metrics all|specific] — single file or stdin analysis. (2) corpus-report <dir> [--output report.csv] — analyze all fixtures, produce comparison table. (3) golden-update — regenerate expected/*.json from current engine output. (4) syllable-bench <wordlist> — test syllable accuracy against known-correct counts. DEPENDENCIES: clap for arg parsing, serde_json for output, csv crate for corpus reports. Depends on readability-core (not the worker). USAGE IN CI: The cross-library comparison script (Python) calls the CLI to get our results, then compares against textstat's results for the same fixtures.

title: Write formula golden tests (fixed-count)
description: Each formula gets tests with hardcoded TextStats inputs and expected outputs. Tests the MATH independent of tokenization. Must cover: correct result, rounding, division-by-zero → Err, boundary values.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Golden tests verify the MATH independent of tokenization. They feed hardcoded TextStats values directly into formula functions and assert exact output. This is the most important test category because it catches coefficient errors, operator precedence bugs, and edge-case mishandling. STRUCTURE: One test function per formula × multiple scenarios. SCENARIOS PER FORMULA: (1) Normal input with known correct output. (2) Boundary values (very short, very long). (3) Edge case: 0 sentences → must return Err(FormulaError::DivisionByZero). (4) Edge case: 0 words → must return Err. (5) Verify rounding behavior (internal full precision, display rounds to options.round decimal places). (6) Verify the SMOG parenthesization: assert 1.043*sqrt(100)+3.1291 ≠ 1.043*(sqrt(100)+3.1291). ASSERTION: Use assert_approx_eq with 1e-9 tolerance for floating point. WHY FIXED-COUNT: If we tested end-to-end (text in → score out), a tokenizer bug would cause formula tests to fail, making root-cause analysis harder. Fixed-count tests isolate formula correctness from tokenizer correctness.

title: Set up insta snapshot tests
description: Every golden fixture text → full API response snapshot checked into git. Catches: schema drift, field ordering changes, type changes, score regressions. Updates require explicit cargo insta review.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: insta snapshot testing catches unintentional API response changes that unit tests miss: new fields appearing, field order changing, types shifting (int→float), values drifting. Every golden fixture gets a .snap file checked into git. IMPLEMENTATION: Use the insta crate. For each fixture in fixtures/texts/: call analyze() with default options, serialize to JSON, assert_json_snapshot!(). Store snapshots in tests/snapshots/. WORKFLOW: When scores change intentionally (e.g., fixing a formula bug), run 'cargo insta review' to inspect and approve the diff. This forces human review of ALL downstream score changes from any code modification. WHY NOT JUST GOLDEN TESTS: Golden tests verify individual formulas with fixed counts. Snapshot tests verify the INTEGRATION — that preprocessing + tokenization + formulas + consensus produce a stable end-to-end result. They also catch schema drift (did someone add a field? remove one? change its type?).

title: Build cross-library comparison harness
description: Python script compares our results against textstat/py-readability-metrics on shared fixtures. Two categories: same fixed counts = exact match; same text + different tokenizer = explainable difference. Generates CSV report.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Cross-library comparison validates our formula implementations against established readability tools (textstat Python, readability-scores npm, py-readability-metrics). If our results diverge significantly from multiple independent implementations, we likely have a bug. Small differences are expected (different syllable algorithms, tokenization choices) but large divergences indicate errors. GOAL: Build a comparison harness that runs the same 30+ fixture texts through our engine AND at least 2 other readability libraries, then compares scores. Acceptable variance: within 0.5 grade levels for grade-based formulas, within 5 points for Flesch Reading Ease. IMPLEMENTATION: A standalone script (Python or Node) that: (1) reads each fixture text file, (2) computes scores via textstat and/or readability-scores, (3) outputs expected ranges as JSON, (4) our Rust golden tests can then assert our scores fall within those ranges. WHY NOT EXACT MATCH: Different libraries make different tokenization and syllable-counting choices. Exact match would mean we copied their bugs. Instead, we want our canonical formula implementations to produce results within the academically-acceptable variance band. WHAT THIS CATCHES: (1) Formula coefficient typos (e.g., using 0.39 instead of 0.0496). (2) Off-by-one in sentence/word counting. (3) Systematic syllable over/under-counting. (4) Wrong interpretation of formula (e.g., Dale-Chall correction factor applied incorrectly). CADENCE: Run comparison after any change to formulas, tokenization, or syllable counting. Not part of CI (requires Python env) but documented as a manual validation step.

title: Write tokenizer edge-case tests
description: 10 specific fixtures: Mr. Smith, U.S., Dr. Jones e.g., 3.14, list items, !!!/???, A.I., HTML entities, Markdown, emojis/smart quotes. Each asserts exact sentence count and word count.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The tokenizer (sentence segmentation + word splitting) is the foundation of all formula computation. Errors here propagate to every single readability score. Edge cases in English text are numerous: abbreviations (Mr., Dr., U.S.A.), decimal numbers (3.14), ellipses (...), URLs, email addresses, quoted speech, parentheticals, em-dashes. GOAL: Build a comprehensive edge-case test suite that exercises every known tokenization ambiguity and verifies correct sentence/word counts. TEST CATEGORIES: (1) Abbreviations: "Mr. Smith went to Washington." = 1 sentence, not 2. "Dr. Jane Smith, Ph.D., spoke." = 1 sentence. (2) Numbers: "The price is $3.14." = 1 sentence. "See sections 2.1 and 2.2." = 1 sentence. (3) Ellipses: "Wait... what?" = 1 sentence (or 2, document our choice). (4) URLs/emails: "Visit example.com. Then go." = 2 sentences (period in URL not sentence boundary). (5) Quotation marks: "'Hello,' she said. 'Goodbye.'" = sentence boundary handling with quotes. (6) Lists: "Items: a) first b) second c) third." = tokenization of enumerated items. (7) Empty/degenerate: "", " ", ".", "...", single word, single character. (8) Unicode: em-dash separated clauses, non-breaking spaces, zero-width chars. STRUCTURE: Each test case is a tuple of (input_text, expected_sentence_count, expected_word_count). Organized by category in separate test functions for clear failure reporting. MAINTENANCE: When a bug is found in production tokenization, add a regression test here FIRST, then fix.

title: Write syllable accuracy tests
description: Rule fixtures, exception fixtures, cache hit/miss verification, hard-word regression list (50+ words with known counts). Target: <5% error rate on standard English corpus.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Syllable counting accuracy directly impacts 5 of our 8 formulas (Flesch, FK, Fog, SMOG, and indirectly Dale-Chall/Spache through complex word identification). Our rule-based estimator + exception dictionary approach is a tradeoff between accuracy and WASM binary size (a full CMU dictionary would add 5MB+). We need to measure and track accuracy against ground truth. GOAL: Build a test suite that measures syllable counting accuracy against the CMU Pronouncing Dictionary (the gold standard for English pronunciation/syllable data). Target: 95%+ exact match rate on common English words. TEST APPROACH: (1) Extract a representative sample of 5000 words from CMU dict with known syllable counts. (2) Run our syllable estimator on each. (3) Report: exact match %, off-by-one %, off-by-two+ %. (4) Track over time -- accuracy should never decrease (ratchet). WORD SELECTION: Stratified sample: 2000 common words (top frequency), 2000 medium-frequency words, 1000 uncommon words. This ensures we measure performance on the words that actually appear in real text, weighted by frequency. FAILURE ANALYSIS: Words where we differ from CMU by 2+ syllables are candidates for the exception dictionary (bead .9). Words where we differ by 1 may be acceptable (CMU reflects pronunciation, not orthographic syllables -- some linguists disagree on edge cases). INTEGRATION: Run as part of cargo test. Results logged as test output. A test FAILS if accuracy drops below 93% (hard floor) or regresses more than 0.5% from the last measured baseline.

title: Write property and fuzz tests
description: No panics on arbitrary UTF-8, no infinite loops, no NaN/Infinity, non-negative counts, empty→error, long single token stays O(n), no regex catastrophic backtrack, batch cross-contamination.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Fuzz testing is critical for a text-processing API that accepts arbitrary user input. We must guarantee: no panics, no infinite loops, no NaN/Infinity in output, no negative counts. IMPLEMENTATION: Use cargo-fuzz or proptest. Generators: (1) arbitrary UTF-8 strings (including emoji, RTL, zero-width chars). (2) strings of only punctuation. (3) very long single 'word' (100KB of 'a'). (4) strings with embedded null bytes. (5) HTML-like strings with unclosed tags. (6) extremely repetitive text (same word 10,000 times). ASSERTIONS: analyze() returns Ok or a well-formed Err — never panics. If Ok: all numeric fields are finite (not NaN/Inf), all counts are ≥ 0, sentences ≥ 1 if words > 0. BATCH ISOLATION: Fuzz a batch where some texts are valid and others are adversarial — verify the adversarial texts don't corrupt results for valid texts. RUN TIME: Fuzz for at least 10 minutes per target in CI. Longer in nightly runs.

title: Set up cargo-mutants for formula code
description: Mutation testing for formulas/ module. Sign flips, constant changes, boundary shifts must all be caught. Zero surviving mutants required. If a mutant survives → test gap, add a test.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Mutation testing verifies that our tests actually CATCH bugs, not just cover lines. cargo-mutants modifies formula code (flip signs, change constants, swap operators) and reruns tests. Any 'surviving mutant' (mutation that doesn't fail a test) reveals a test gap. SCOPE: Focus on readability-core/src/formulas/ — these are pure functions with exact mathematical contracts, making them ideal mutation targets. EXAMPLES OF MUTATIONS: (1) 206.835 → 207.835 in Flesch. (2) - → + in subtraction. (3) 1.015 → 1.015 * 2. (4) > 5 → > 6 in Dale-Chall threshold. Each of these MUST be caught by existing golden tests. If not, the golden test set is incomplete. TARGET: Zero surviving mutants in formulas/ module. WHY THIS MATTERS: Code coverage can be 100% while tests are weak ('covers the line but doesn't verify the output'). Mutation testing measures test STRENGTH, not just coverage.

title: Write Worker integration tests
description: Wrangler/Miniflare tests: valid request, missing/invalid csvkey→401, empty→422, too large→413, metric subset, CORS preflight (no auth), health (no auth), ETag→304, batch mixed, scores_only format, no text in logs, X-Request-Id header.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Worker integration tests validate the full HTTP request/response cycle: auth checking, request parsing, readability computation, response serialization, error handling, and CORS headers. Unlike unit tests (which test readability-core in isolation), these tests exercise the complete Worker pipeline including WASM compilation. GOAL: Test the Worker as a black box via HTTP requests, verifying correct behavior for all endpoints and error conditions. TEST FRAMEWORK: Use wrangler dev (local mode) or miniflare for programmatic testing. Tests send real HTTP requests and assert on response status, headers, and body shape. TEST CASES: (1) Auth: valid csvkey succeeds, missing csvkey returns 401, wrong csvkey returns 401. (2) POST /analyze: valid text returns 200 with expected JSON shape, empty text returns 400, oversized text returns 413. (3) POST /analyze/batch: array of 1-10 texts succeeds, empty array returns 400, over 10 texts returns 400, mixed valid/invalid texts returns per-item errors. (4) GET /health: returns 200 with { status: "ok" }. (5) CORS: OPTIONS returns preflight headers, POST response includes CORS headers. (6) Format param: ?format=compact returns compact shape, ?format=scores_only returns minimal shape. (7) ETag: same text returns same ETag, If-None-Match with matching ETag returns 304. (8) Method errors: GET /analyze returns 405. ENVIRONMENT: Tests run against wrangler dev with CSVKEY=test-key-12345. NOT run in CI initially (requires wrangler), but documented for local validation.

title: Phase 5: Production Hardening
description: Final production polish: CPU budget with partial results, cache tuning, performance benchmarks, observability, documentation, and deployment guide.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: PHASE OVERVIEW: Hardening takes a working, tested system and prepares it for production traffic. This includes: performance optimization (CPU budget enforcement, cache tuning, arena allocation benchmarking), production observability (structured metrics, diagnostic headers), security hardening (no-text-logging verification, panic containment), and operational documentation (deployment guide, README). WHY SEPARATE FROM PHASE 3-4: The system must be correct before it can be optimized. Premature optimization in Phases 2-3 would complicate implementation and testing. Phase 5 assumes correctness and focuses on non-functional requirements: latency, memory usage, debuggability, operability. KEY OUTPUTS: (1) CPU budget with partial results -- the 30ms cap that returns whatever formulas completed rather than timing out entirely. (2) Tuned syllable cache with measured hit rates. (3) Arena allocation validated with benchmarks. (4) Observability headers and structured logs. (5) Sentinel test proving no user text appears in logs (privacy compliance). (6) Criterion benchmark baselines for regression detection. (7) README with API documentation. (8) Deployment guide from git clone to production. DEPENDENCY: Requires all of Phases 1-4. Some beads (like cache tuning) require the fixture corpus and test infrastructure from Phase 4 to generate realistic workloads.

title: Implement CPU budget with partial results
description: 30ms default budget. Check elapsed time between formula computations. If exceeded: return completed scores, mark remaining as valid:false error:TIMEOUT, add PARTIAL_RESULT warning. HTTP 200 (partial success is useful).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Cloudflare Workers have strict CPU time limits (10-50ms depending on plan). A pathological input (e.g., 100KB of text with unusual Unicode) could exceed this, causing Cloudflare to terminate the Worker. The CPU budget prevents this by returning partial results gracefully. IMPLEMENTATION: Record start time (performance.now() equivalent in WASM). Between each formula computation, check elapsed time. If > 30ms (configurable): stop computing formulas, mark unfinished ones as valid:false, error:'TIMEOUT'. Add PARTIAL_RESULT top-level warning. Return HTTP 200 (partial success is useful — better than 500). FORMULA ORDER: Run formulas in priority order: Flesch-Kincaid, Flesch, Coleman-Liau, ARI (all fast, Tier 0/1), then Gunning Fog, SMOG (Tier 1), then Dale-Chall, Spache (Tier 2, most expensive). This maximizes the number of scores returned before a timeout. CONSIDERATION: The consensus algorithm must handle partial results — if only 3 of 8 formulas completed, it uses the 3-4 metric rule (median). Label confidence as 'low' when using partial data.

title: Tune syllable LRU cache
description: Benchmark cache at 1024/2048/4096 entries. Measure hit rate on golden corpus. Verify lookup cost < recomputation for short words. Target ≥70% hit rate. May need to pre-seed with top-100 English words.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: The syllable LRU cache (Phase 2 bead .10) ships with a default size of 2048 entries. This may not be optimal -- too small wastes computation on evicted entries, too large wastes memory in the WASM heap. Cache tuning requires production-like workloads to measure real hit rates. GOAL: Measure cache performance under realistic workloads and determine if 2048 is the right capacity. Adjust if data shows a clearly better value. METHODOLOGY: (1) Instrument the cache with hit/miss counters (debug builds only, zero-cost in release). (2) Run the fixture corpus (30+ texts of varying difficulty and length) through the engine, recording hit rate at different capacities: 512, 1024, 2048, 4096. (3) Also measure: unique words per typical request, cache warm-up curve (how many requests until steady-state hit rate). EXPECTED FINDINGS: English text follows Zipf's law -- a small vocabulary covers most occurrences. The top 2000 words likely cover 95%+ of all word tokens in typical text. So 2048 should achieve near-maximum hit rate for single requests. The benefit of larger caches is cross-request sharing: subsequent requests benefit from vocabulary cached by earlier ones. DECISION CRITERIA: If 4096 improves hit rate by less than 2% over 2048 in multi-request scenarios, keep 2048 (memory matters in WASM). If 1024 achieves within 1% of 2048, consider downsizing. MEMORY MATH: Each entry is approximately 24 bytes (word String avg 10 chars + 8 byte String overhead + 1 byte count + LRU node overhead ~5 bytes). 2048 entries = ~48KB. 4096 = ~96KB. Both are well within WASM budget.

title: Benchmark arena allocator impact
description: Compare bumpalo arena vs standard alloc for tokenizer work. Measure on 500-word and 5000-word texts. If <10% improvement, remove bumpalo dep to reduce complexity. WASM linear memory makes this worth testing.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Arena allocation via bumpalo gives us bump-pointer allocation for per-request temporary data (tokenizer output, intermediate word lists, per-paragraph stats). The arena is reset at request end, making deallocation O(1) regardless of how many allocations occurred. This is critical for consistent latency in a Worker where we cannot afford GC pauses. GOAL: Benchmark arena allocation performance to validate that it provides meaningful speedup over standard allocation, and to determine optimal arena pre-allocation size. BENCHMARKS: (1) Baseline: standard Vec allocations for tokenizer output (words, sentences). (2) Arena: same operations using bumpalo::Bump with pre-allocated capacity. (3) Measure: allocation time, peak memory, total request latency. Run across fixture corpus (small 50-word text, medium 500-word, large 5000-word). WHAT WE EXPECT: Arena should show 2-5x speedup for allocation-heavy paths (tokenizer creates many small String/str slices). The benefit increases with text size (more allocations amortized). PRE-ALLOCATION SIZE: Start with 16KB arena (covers ~5000 word pointers + sentence boundaries for typical text). Benchmark will validate or adjust this. TOO SMALL: arena grows (still fast, just one extra allocation for backing storage). TOO LARGE: wasted memory on small requests. 16KB is a reasonable middle ground. TOOLING: criterion benchmarks in readability-core/benches/. Criterion provides statistical significance testing, so we can confidently detect regressions. WASM NOTE: bumpalo works on wasm32 (no OS allocator dependency). The WASM global allocator (dlmalloc or wee_alloc) handles the arena's backing storage allocation.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .6 (criterion benchmarks). Arena vs std-alloc comparison is just one scenario within the comprehensive benchmark suite. No need for a separate bead.

title: Implement structured observability logging
description: console_log! JSON per request: request_id, path, status, duration_ms, bytes, words, sentences, metrics, response_format, etag_hit, batch_size, warnings. Parseable by Cloudflare Logpush.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Production observability goes beyond request logging (Phase 3 bead .9). It includes: performance monitoring (p50/p95/p99 latencies), error rate tracking, cache effectiveness metrics, and alerting thresholds. Cloudflare Workers provide Analytics Engine and Workers Analytics for this, but we need to emit the right data points. GOAL: Implement metrics emission that enables dashboarding and alerting without external dependencies (no Datadog SDK in WASM). Use CF-native capabilities. METRICS TO TRACK: (1) Request latency histogram (total request time, computation time, serialization time). (2) Text size distribution (bytes and word count). (3) Formula computation breakdown (which formulas succeed/fail/skip per request). (4) Cache metrics (syllable cache hit rate per request, cumulative). (5) Error rate by type (auth failures, validation errors, computation errors, panics). (6) CPU budget usage (what percentage of 30ms budget was consumed). IMPLEMENTATION OPTIONS: (1) CF Analytics Engine (structured data points, queryable via GraphQL). (2) CF Logpush (stream structured logs to R2/external). (3) Custom headers (X-Timing, X-Cache-Hit-Rate) for client-side monitoring. We implement option 3 (custom response headers) as minimum viable observability, with structured log lines (from bead .9) as the data source for options 1-2. CUSTOM HEADERS: X-Processing-Time-Ms, X-Word-Count, X-Formulas-Computed, X-Cache-Hit-Rate (0.0-1.0). These are opt-in via ?verbose=true query param (not exposed by default to keep responses clean).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into Phase 3 .9 (structured logging). The observability headers (X-Processing-Time-Ms etc.) are trivially added in the same logging module. The distinction between 'basic logging' and 'observability' was artificial — it is all one coherent request instrumentation concern implemented in the same code path.

title: Write no-text-logging sentinel test
description: Submit text containing unique sentinel string. Capture all console_log output. Assert sentinel NEVER appears in logs. This is the privacy gate — if it fails, we're leaking user text.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: This is the PRIVACY GATE. If this test fails, we are leaking user-submitted text in logs/errors. It must be a hard CI failure. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Choose a unique sentinel string that would never appear naturally: e.g., 'SENTINEL_READABILITY_PRIVACY_CHECK_7f3a2b'. (2) Submit it as text to the Worker (or call analyze() in test mode with log capture). (3) Capture ALL console_log output, error messages, analytics payloads. (4) Assert the sentinel string NEVER appears in captured output. (5) Also check: text_hash appears (we DO log hashes), but the raw text does NOT. WHAT THIS CATCHES: Accidental debug logging that includes request body, error messages that quote the input, analytics that store text snippets. WHY SENTINEL: A random UUID-like string is unmistakable — if it appears in logs, it could ONLY have come from the submitted text being logged.

title: Run criterion benchmarks, arena comparison, and set baselines
description: Scenarios: 100w (≤2ms), 500w (≤5ms), 5000w (≤10ms), 25000w (≤50ms), 10-text batch (≤50ms), pathological 128KB (no panic). Also benchmark metrics:[coleman_liau] vs metrics:[all] to validate lazy computation (≥30% faster). ARENA: Compare bumpalo arena vs standard alloc for tokenizer on 500w and 5000w texts — if <10% improvement, remove bumpalo dep. CACHE: syllable cache warm vs cold comparison. CI gate: >10% regression from stabilized baseline = failure.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: criterion benchmarks establish performance baselines and CI regression gates. They validate that our architecture decisions (tiered computation, LRU cache, arena allocation) actually deliver their promised speedups. SCENARIOS (from PRD §19.10): 100-word (≤2ms), 500-word (≤5ms), 5000-word (≤10ms), 25000-word (≤50ms), 10-text batch (≤50ms), 128KB pathological (no panic). CRITICAL BENCHMARK: metrics:[coleman_liau] vs metrics:[all] — must show ≥30% speedup to validate tiered computation. If it doesn't, the tier architecture is wasted complexity and should be simplified. ADDITIONAL: syllable cache warm vs cold comparison, arena vs standard alloc comparison (if arena benchmarks from .3 show <10% improvement, remove bumpalo). CI GATE: > 10% regression from stabilized baseline = CI failure. This prevents gradual perf drift. REPORTING: criterion generates HTML reports — store in CI artifacts for visual comparison.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .3 (arena benchmarks). Arena vs std-alloc comparison is one scenario within the comprehensive benchmark suite, not a standalone task.

title: Write README, API documentation, and deployment guide
description: Covers: what it does, quick start, csvkey setup, local dev, request/response examples for all 3 formats, batch usage, metrics, warnings, interpretations, privacy policy, limitations, disclaimer. DEPLOYMENT SECTION: prerequisites (Rust, wrangler, CF account), wrangler secret put CSVKEY, wrangler deploy, preview/staging/production environments, rollback via CF versioning, monitoring with wrangler tail. Single coherent documentation artifact — README.md at repo root with deployment as a dedicated section.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: Documentation is the product's first impression. The README must enable a developer to go from zero to deployed in <15 minutes. STRUCTURE: (1) One-paragraph description. (2) Quick start: curl example with csvkey. (3) Response interpretation: what does a score of 8.9 mean? (4) All three response formats with examples. (5) Batch endpoint usage. (6) Supported metrics table with formula references. (7) Warning codes and their meaning. (8) Setup: wrangler secret put CSVKEY, wrangler deploy. (9) Local dev: cargo test, wrangler dev. (10) Privacy: we don't log your text. (11) Performance characteristics. (12) Known limitations. (13) Required disclaimer: 'Readability formulas are estimates. They do not measure factual accuracy, tone, audience fit, inclusivity, persuasion, legal sufficiency, medical safety, or comprehension by a specific reader.' WHY THIS MATTERS: An API without docs is an API nobody uses. The disclaimer protects against liability claims from users who equate 'grade 8' with 'legally compliant plain language'.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGE NOTE: This bead absorbed former bead .8 (deployment guide). For a single-Worker project, deployment is a README section, not a separate document.

title: Write deployment and rollback guide
description: Document: wrangler secret put CSVKEY, wrangler deploy, preview environments, staging→production promotion, rollback via Cloudflare versioning. Stateless = safe rollback always.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CONTEXT: A deployment guide ensures that anyone (including future-us) can take the code from git clone to production Worker in a predictable, repeatable way. CF Workers deployment has several non-obvious steps (secret binding, route configuration, custom domain setup) that are easy to forget. GOAL: Document the complete deployment lifecycle from local development through staging to production. CONTENTS: (1) Prerequisites: Rust toolchain, wrangler CLI, Cloudflare account with Workers paid plan. (2) Local development: wrangler dev with --var CSVKEY:local-test-key, testing with curl. (3) Secret setup: wrangler secret put CSVKEY (production value from team vault). (4) First deploy: wrangler deploy (creates worker on workers.dev subdomain). (5) Custom domain: CF dashboard route configuration or wrangler.toml routes. (6) Staging environment: wrangler deploy --env staging. (7) Rollback: wrangler rollback (instant, zero-downtime). (8) Monitoring post-deploy: wrangler tail for live logs, CF dashboard for analytics. (9) Updating: git pull, wrangler deploy (build happens remotely on CF). FORMAT: Markdown document in /docs/deployment.md with numbered steps and copy-paste commands. Include expected output for verification steps. MAINTENANCE: Update this guide whenever wrangler.toml changes, new secrets are added, or deployment process evolves. This is a living document, not a one-time artifact.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED into .7 (README/docs). Deployment is a section within the README, not a separate document. For a single-Worker project with no complex infra, splitting deployment from the main docs creates unnecessary fragmentation.
